blocks.text: Application issues: Climate novels, disaster/migration narratives, marine literature, energy and mining literature, toxicity and waste, multi-species narratives, environmental transmission. Limitations: scale leaps, disaster aestheticization, indigenous knowledge as a resource, North climate theory universal, short-term experiments as an alternative to political action. The strongest design is the four parallel layers of “History Responsibility - Physical Infrastructure - Form Strategy - Reader/Community Consequences”.
5.4 Theoretical institutional insulation: planetary scales must be established in conjunction with differentiated responsibilities

Conceptual distinction. The term “global” often refers to the space of human relations made up of capital, empires and technologies, while the term “planetary” refers to Earth systems that are not centred on human purposes. Dipesh Chakrabarty stressed in * The climate of Histoory in a Planety Age* that both scales must be taken together: climate change requires human beings to be treated as a force of species, and historical justice requires a distinction between class, empire and colonial responsibility. Core mechanisms. Literary can shift between person time, intergenerational time, geological time, turning hard-to-understood chronic damage, energy dependence and multi-species relationships into narratives; but “human life” will not remain in the grand metaphor unless it enters land, mining, oceans, plantations, militarization and archival choices. Kathryn Yusoff *A Billion Black Anthropones or None* illustrates how geological classification, resource extraction and the reversibility of black life together form modern geological knowledge; Max Liboison *POLITION Is Colonialism* shifts pollution from material over-marking to continuous occupation of indigenous lands and determining who can be damaged. Most critical. The abstract term “humans” can evenize those who create and withstand crises; in turn, it can reduce literature to illustrations of the topic if only the work is labelled as climatic, marine or multi-species. Empirical ecological criticism, if measured only attitudinal change, also reduced literary values to short-term behavioural effects. The border applies. The planetary concept cannot be a substitute for local languages, land law, colonial history and community knowledge; participatory environmental research must describe data ownership, feedback and denial rights. Microcases. *Allegories of the Anthropocene* linking sea level, nuclear testing, enslaved routes and imperial visual systems through the Caribbean and Pacific Islands text show that “islands” are not natural symbols of vulnerability, but rather observations of how global environmental responsibilities are spatialized.
5.4.1 From novel form to digital interface, then to media material chain

Amitav Ghosh, The Great Disorder: Climate Change and Unimaginable*, points out that the probabilities, credibility and everyday norms of modern serious novels can make extreme weather events “unrealistic”; this diagnosis does not mean that all realism is unable to express a climate crisis by extending forms to imperial history and politics. Anna Lowenhaupt Tsing, Jennifer Deger, Alder Keleman Saxona, and Feifei Zhou, co-authored/edited by Anna Lowenhaupt Zing, and their network of contributors, cannot be reduced to a single author of four planners. (Tsing et al., 2020) Cajetan Iheka ' s African Ecomedia: Networking Forms and Planetary Politics (*Africa Ecodeia: Network Forms, Planetary Policy*) further calls for putting the environmental crisis represented by the media in the same analytical framework as the chain of minerals, energy, labour and electronic waste on which the media itself relies. (Iheka, 2021)

Three cases responded, respectively, to “what form is considered credible” “how the interface organizes knowledge” “what the media is based on” “the physical chain”. Environmental literature research should therefore not be stopped in thematic identification, but should be accompanied by a simultaneous analysis of text forms, publishing/interfaces and production-discard footprints.
Chapter 6 Post-colonial, decolonizing and global South
6.1 From post-colonial criticism to decolonization practice

The spectrum is not linear: anti-colonialism and national liberation, post-colonial manifestations/talks/mixed criticisms, modernity/colonization, aboriginal sovereignty and settlement colonial criticism, freedom of perception, Southern approaches and reworlding. Magolo/Walsh has made decolonisation a concept, analysis and practice (Mignolo et al., 2018); Smith has rewritten the Aboriginal research relationship, agenda and project governance approach (Smith, 2021); Tuck/Yang insists that “decolonization is not a metaphor”, reminding diversity courses, inclusive language and land restitution are not the same thing (Tuck et al., 2012).

Africa's “freedom of understanding” (estimetic freedom) emphasizes the issue not only of joining the African dimension, but of decolonizing knowledge classification, discipline and publication centres (Ndlovu-Gatsheni, 2018). Ndlovu-Gatsheni advanced from critical colonial world models to re-establishing relationships in 2023 with “reworlding from the Global South” (Ndlovu-Gatsheni, 2023). This echoes the methodologicalized version of China: instead of replacing Western universality with “China's specialty”, it is the production of knowledge in a complex manner in China's relations with Asia, Africa and global capitalism (Li et al., 2023).
6.2 New Targets and Debates in the 2020s

Whether the Global South is a relational location or a geographic container: It may refer to positions in the empire, capital and intellectual hierarchy, but it has internal power of the State, class, caste, ethnic group and gender. The unification of the South would leave the elite of the post-colonial countries impersonating subaltern (Ahmad, 2023; Ndlovu-Gatsheni, 2023).

The sports texts are theoretical: Ahmad studied the 1970s booklet banned in the Baluchistan region of Pakistan, suggesting that anti-colonial ideas were often collective, anonymous, underground and fragmented; access to them required “anti-infrastructure” archival methods and also had to address the ethical relationship between academia and political struggle (Ahmad, 2023).

Multilingual practice: Language mixing is not irregular, but rather may be a colonial intellectual production; but the celebration of mobility cannot ignore the language punishment of schools, publications and platforms (Chaka 2020).

The struggle between colonization and “de-Westernization”: a new set of classics or local cases is only curriculum pluralism; decolonization also involves land, ownership, data, citation, translation, financing and co-governance (Smith, 2021; Tucker et al., 2012).
The tension of localism and comparison: China as Method has a decentrized power only when exceptionalism, relationshipization and South-South engagement are avoided (Li et al., 2023).
6.3 Methodological tool chain
Issue governance: Research on who issues are raised, who uses them, and in what language results are returned; signature of dynamic consent, signature and access agreements (Smith, 2021).

Multilingual reading and translation spectrum: preservation of original language, sound translation, translation, interpretation; non-translation of records and differences in versions, without using English translation as a transparent double (Chaka 2020; Apter, 2013).

(c) Debris file retrieval: brochures, private collections, oral transmissions, leaflets, songs, prison texts, underground periodicals and overseas copy exchange certificates; missing records are the result of violence rather than “no thought” (Ahmad, 2023).

South-South comparisons: first to determine comparable relationships (colonial regimes, ocean corridors, translation chains, platform infrastructure) and then to compare; to avoid using the Euro-American theory as a default common denominator (Ndlovu-Gatsheni, 2023; Li, etc., 2023).

Quoting and system auditing: statistical theoretical sources, author location, language of publication, cost of access and research funding; quantitative results must return to content and structure, without a quota of nationality.
6.4 Representation of results and available boundaries

rows: Research as a colonial history and indigenous project
Upgrading from ethical clearance to common governance and return of results
Curriculum diversity, decentrization of knowledge, land/sovereign action, disaggregated in the report
Combining conceptual history and practice cases, not just a terminological overview
Research on African knowledge production structures and Southern world generation
Studying banning journals, collective signatures, oral — printing cycles and file violence
China-Mozambique, China-Asia, etc. Relationship cases, not ethnic exceptions

blocks.text: Questions of application: Anti-colonial literature, First Nations literature, cross-linguistic writing, imperial archives, South-South circulation, curriculum/citation politics, digital cultural sovereignty. Limitations: moral labelling of terms; certification of “knowledge of the South” with the Northern Academic Evaluation System; neglect of internal oppression in the South; lack of language skills for researchers to speak out; harm caused by the disclosure of sensitive archives. The most stringent innovation criterion in this area is whether research has changed the system of evidence and relationships, rather than simply changing the object.
6.5 Theoretical mechanisms: from expression of criticism to redistribution of research relationships

Conceptual distinction. Post-colonial criticism mainly analyses imperial discourse, manifestations and multiplicity; decolonization refers to colonization that continues to operate after political independence in the intellectual, labour, race and subject categories; and decolonization also involves land, sovereignty and institutional restitution in the settlement colonial context. The three are relevant, but they cannot be replaced. Core mechanisms. Walter D. Mignolo and Catherine E. Walsh in *On Decoration: Concepts, Analytics, Praxis* link decolonisation organizations to concepts, analysis and practice; Linda Tuhiwai Smith's * Declonizing Methodologies: Research and Indigenous Peoples, 3rd ed.* addresses the question to whom questions are asked, who keeps the material, who explains, who benefits and who can refuse. Theoretical mechanisms are therefore not “a different set of classics”, but rather a redistribution of research agendas, language work, file control, signature and feedback on results. Most critical. Eve Tuck and K. Wayne Yang, in “Designation Is not a Metaphor”, noted that when all anti-discrimination or curriculum pluralism is named colonial, land and sovereignty claims will disappear again. Another risk is to imagine the “global South” as a unified and pure body of knowledge, ignoring the power of the State, caste, class and gender within the local context. The border applies. The curriculum expansion, decentrization, data governance and land restitution should be presented separately and not replaced by one; nor should researchers exempt evidence and counter-tests on grounds of respect for the place. Microcases. Mahvish Ahmad, in the “Movement Texts as Anti-Colonial Theory”, described the banning of magazines, leaflets, group signatures and debris files as theoretical production rather than raw materials for the concept of the college, demonstrating how the anti-colonial text constitutes knowledge through dissemination, risk and co-writing itself.
6.5.1 “Global South” is a political spectrum and not a homogenous area on the map

Anne Garland Mahler, From Three Continents to the Global South: Race, Radicalism and Transnational Solidarity, traced the Global South to the 1966 Havana Three Continents Movement, Black Internationalism, Multilingual Cultural Production and Transnational Solidarity. This means that the concept is first named by the place of relations between colonial history and political alliances, rather than by the list of countries of the southern hemisphere. The design of the study should indicate, respectively, whether the historical movement, the contemporary knowledge-producing coalition, the unequal place in the world system or the geographical area were discussed, and whether the “global South” would be reduced from an analytical concept to a moral label if used in combination. This spectrum also does not eliminate caste, class, state, gender and ethnic oppression within the South.
Chapter 7 New phase of study of world literature and translation
7.1 Theory spectrometry and current shift
The contemporary world literature debate can be modelled on four mutually constrained models:
`text flow/outbound reading - ┐ world system/centre - margin scale - → multiscale, transregional, ocean, archipelagic, multilingual, untranslable and translation politics - `Apter' - `Cheah' - `

Damrosch defines world literature by the circulation and reading of works that have gone beyond their original culture (Damrosch, 2003); Apter points out that large-scale paradigms make market readability universal and require serious consideration of non-relationability, misinterpretation and geopolitical resistance (Apter, 2013); Walkowitz's “borm translation” considers translation as a contemporary fictional and formal condition (Walkowitz, 2015); Cheah rejects literature as a global commodity flow only, emphasizing the ability to start alternative times and worlds (Cheah, 2016).

The integrated orientation of the 2020s is a multi-scale world literature: countries are still useful, but need to be analysed with multilingual places, regions, oceans, islands, migration, publishing chains and digital platforms. Cambridge World Literature History uses transregional universalization, map transfer, translation, media, circulation and planetary organizations as the same methodological framework (Debjani Ganguly (ed. ), 2021); Cambridge World Literature Guide can be used to combe concepts and academic differences (Etherington et al., 2018).
7.2 New disputes

Whether circulation is equal to value: translated, awarded and enrolled courses are co-screened by publishers, agents, festivals, grants and English visibility, not the “world nature” inherent in the text.

Translation is a link and a mechanism of discrimination: it can expand accessibility and can also create differential treatment through accents, identity labels and asymmetrical resources (Angermeyer, 2023).

Academic languages are not consistent with the target language: the language of expression chosen by translators is influenced by the structure of profession, periodicals and visibility; studies of English meta-studies underestimate studies of native languages (Li, 2019).

Non-translation is not a stop-to-comparison: it requires the preservation of the history, language and political friction of words, using notes, simultaneous translations and multiple translations, rather than the quick search for a single equivalent (Apter, 2013).

World Literature and Planetary Crisis: The codification of world history began to integrate environmental disasters, migration and humanitarian crises into the scale, linking environmental humanities (Debjani Ganguly (ed. ), 2021; Chakrabarty, 2021).
7.3 Methodological tool chain

Multi-translation detail: selection of original text of the same paragraph, translation from different ages/regions, comparison of grammar, sound, cultural words, deletion, annotated and review; recording of translation sequences and editorial decisions.

Publication and by-text studies: contracts, cover, waist covers, book reviews, awards, festivals, courses and marketing materials to re-establish the institutional path of “becoming world literature”.
Circulation networks: Author-translator-publishing-language-city maps; nodals do not equal aesthetic values, need to check data missing and large publishing group effects.
Multiscale comparison: The same is read separately at multilingual local, regional and global scales, identifying what is seen/covered at each scale (Debjani Ganguly (ed.), 2021).
Translation of national identity: interview interpreter, editor, agent and reader; study of remuneration, time, risk and invisible work.
Digital Bibliography: ISBN, WorldCat, National Library, publisher catalogue and book-review database cross-certification; no global circulation is declared in a single English-language database.
7.4 Representative Outcomes and Case Recommendations

rows: How the works are reread and rewritten in different places.
Why can't certain works be made into circulation?
Complete reconstruction of the quantitative structure of the publishing market
How to Pre-relationship, Multilingual and Versions of Contemporary Novels
Translation mechanisms for all historical periods
How literature generates non-capitalist time/relationship worlds
Experience measure the audience's acceptance of the world.
Language-specific insulations need to be done by regional experts
Inequality between institutional translation and academic languages
The literary form analysis needs to be supplemented by a textual approach

blocks.text: Application issues: classical translation, South-South flows, novels in languages, literary awards, publishing markets, ocean/regional comparisons, global writing on digital platforms. Limitations: English-language literature is biased; sales/translation numbers are valued; translation work is ignored; translations are compared only to the original language; and “global” samples are actually from a few capitals. High-quality projects should contain at least one text chain, one institutional chain and one reader/circulation chain.
7.5 Theoretical mechanisms: The world is in the process of changing the work Medium

Conceptual distinction. World literature is not “the sum of the names of countries”. David Damrosch, in *What Is World Limited?* defines it as the mode of circulation and reading of a work that acquires a new life beyond its original language; Emily Apter's *Against World Limited: On the Policies of Untranslatability* reminds that not being translated is not a technical failure, but a moment when language, sovereignty and conceptual differences refuse to be exchanged for equal value; and Pheng Cheah further interprets “the world” in *What Is a World? On Postcolonial Literation as World Limited* as a literary ability to open up relationships and time, not market coverage. Core mechanisms. The entry of works into the world is not simple: the author may pre-set a multilingual reader, the interpreter reorganizes sound and allegations, the publisher selects the packaging of articles, awards and courses give visibility and comment on the Community ' s redefinition of its meaning. * Born Transladed: The Interpretation Novel in an Age of World Limited* proposes “relationship” as an indication that translation can become a formal condition at the creation stage. Most critical. Circulation theory can easily equate access to the English-language market with worldism, and it is irrelevant to misrepresent literature that is not translated; if it is absolute, it can also bring comparison, teaching and cross-linguistic publicity to a standstill. The border applies. The research must be accompanied by the preservation of the original language, the translation spectrum, the interpreter, the publisher, the funding, the receiving language and the uncirculation of material, and cannot be justified by mere sales or awards. Microcases. The “Translation as Documentation: Socialistics and Intelligence in Multilendingual Institutions” of Philip Sebastian Angermeyer reveals that translation also distributes credibility and power in the system; it prompts world literary research to include “who can be understood, who speaks and what languages are used as originals” in the chain of circulation of works to move from text travel to translation politics.
7.5.1 Opposal of literary capital models to critical global English reading

The original French-language book La République mondiale des Lettres*, by Pascal Casanova, describes the unequal world literary space with literary capital, centre-marginal competition, translation and autonomy. The strength of this is to transform “worldwide” into a relationship model, and the weakness is that Parisian centralism, single literary time and national frameworks may again level the multiplicity of central histories. Amir Rashid Mufti, Forget English! Orientalism and Universal Literature!: Orientalisms and World Sciences* from the global English and Orientalist knowledge system ask: What works must first be readable, classified and taught by publishing houses in order to be recognized as “world literature”? (Mufti, 2016)

The two should be read in parallel with the reverse model: the former reveals the distribution of literary capital, while the latter reveals the language and knowledge systems that have been naturally applied to measure such distribution. This would support “the relationship between world literature and power structures”, but would not be able to introduce a rejection of all English writing or to classify all translations as violence.
Chapter 8 Emotional, emotional and “post-critical” contests
8.1 Conceptual spectrometry

At least three lines within the research of emotional movements are non-interchangeable: Spinoza-Deleuze body capacity/relationship intensity, Tomkins-Sedgwick shame/float system and restorative reading, public perception/emotional structure in cultural research and political economy. The Fulfilling Theory of the Fulfillment itself uses a multispectral “list” rather than a uniform definition of organizational areas (Greg et al., 2010). Sedgwick, in distinguishing between “paranoid reading” and “reparative reading”, promotes literary research to reflect whether exposure is the only critical gesture (Sedgwick, 2003).

Berlant's "cruel optimism" examines why people are attached to people who actually impede their prosperity and put their emotions in crisis, class and public culture (Berlant, 2011). In the 2020s, two seemingly opposite and complementary directions emerged: Felski, in order to identify, reconcile, explain the research of art attachments, asked for an explanation of how the work connects the reader with society (Felski 2020); Brinkema ' s radical formalism, in turn, refused to equate the emotional with the subjective perception of the audience, reading the logic of the nonpersonal form from the diagram, grid, rhythm, void and threshold (Brinkema, 2022).

The Fulfilling Theory Book 2 brings Black, Quer/Trans, Indigenous, Algorithmic Governance, Media Ecology and Climate Emergencies to the forefront, showing that the field has expanded from “body strength” to ethnic sensibility, trans-mediarous public, algorithmic trauma and environmental climate (Seigworth et al., 2023). Ngai's contradictory judgement of gimpick — both laborious and inefficient, outdated and over-future — connects aesthetic emotions to forms of capitalist values (Ngai, 2020).
8.2 Core contentions

Whether affect and emotion are divided: pre-set affect as a pre-linguistic, generalized body strength, easily ignoring language, race, gender and history; and call everything anamotion without conscious, atmosphere and relationship analysis. Operational definitions should be clarified and not inverted (Gregg et al., 2010; Seigworth et al., 2023).

Whether the latter criticism abandons politics: not to remove ideological criticism, restorative reading and attachment research increases the causal chain of attraction, consolation, dependence and use of works; it still has to track who gets what attachment (Sedgwick, 2003; Felski, 2020).

The emotional verbs are the content or the form: mere emotional verbs miss rhythm, perspective, pause and layout; mere verbs may ignore the actual reader. Brinkema and the empirically accepted study should form a testable dialogue (Brinkema, 2022; Hugentobler et al., 2021).

Whether negative perceptions are natural resistance: shame, anger, anxiety, fatigue can be mobilized by capital and platforms; “strong feelings” cannot be called political liberation directly (Berlant, 2011; Ngai, 2020).
8.3 Methodology and Case

Form - Retrospect: Mark rhythm, metametry, repetition, perspective, colour, sound, graphs and proxies; how they organize the potential for feeling rather than replacing the reader's statement (Brinkema, 2022).
Reader's diary/interview: Record attachment, exclusion, rereading and life-related links during reading; analyze the path of “identify-coordinate-interpret” (Felski, 2020).

Experiment/speaker: Emotional Dictionary, EFF/Awakening Rating and Physiological Indicators need to be associated with speech models, open answers and cross-reading; irony, free and indirect quotations, historical terms are the main source of error.

(b) The emotional identity: study of the atmosphere in the classroom, the fan circle, protests, platforms and care networks; document the body and location of researchers, but do not treat self-perception as the sole evidence.

Political-economic analysis: linking anxiety, hope, fatigue with labour, debt, platform participation and commodity forms to test how a person who is in love can both sustain and harm life (Berlant, 2011; Ngai, 2020).

rows: Solace, surprise, spell and survival from trauma text
Upswing narratives, romantic comedy, labour/education commitments
A fan rereads, classical teaching, cross-media obsession.
Reader 's Diaries + Nationalities + Analysis of Works
Terror, violence, tabulation, pattern and rhythm
Very close reading + medium form analysis
Platform walk-in + interviews + critical data studies
AI writing, best-selling forms, cultural goods
Aesthetic judgement history plus labour/value analysis

blocks.text: The application problem: reading attachment, public perception, fan studies, crisis narratives, algorithmic atmosphere, aesthetic judgment. Limitations: The concept is expanding indefinitely, the body is becoming universal, researchers replace readers' feelings, the dictionaries are being moved with compassion, and any negative feelings are being romanticized into resistance. It would be useful to identify three levels of “potential emotional movement of text organization” “respondence reported by readers” “measured physical/behavioral reactions”.
8.4 Theoretical mechanisms: attachment is both aesthetic and power relations

Conceptual distinction. “Emotion” usually refers to the sense of being named and socially identifiable, with “affect” emphasizing changes in body capacity, intensity of relationships and changes that have not yet been fully captured in the language; and “postrique” not to stop criticism, but to question whether exposure, suspicion, and cynic interpretation should be the only reading gesture. Melissa Gregg and Gregory J. Seigworth, eds. *The effect Theory Reader* itself defines the area of the organization in a multispectral rather than a uniform way. Core mechanisms. In moving forward to track how feelings flow between bodies, objects, narratives and the public system, Lauren Berlant, in *Cruel Optimism*, defined “cruel optimism” as the object of the subject's attachment as precisely preventing its prosperity, thereby linking aesthetic attachment to labour, class and “good life” commitments. Eve Kosofsky Sedgwick in *Touching Feeing: Act, Pedagogy, Performability* distinguishes paranoia from restorative reading, while Rita Felski *Hooked: Art and Attachment* treats “strucked by the work” as a relationship to be described, not an error to be confused. Most critical. To describe passion as a purely pre-linguistic strength could erase language, history and institutions; the latter criticism, if it were merely a compliment of attachment, could also privateize the plasticization of race, gender and capital. The border applies. Readers ' own statements, platform footprints and text forms must be cross-cutting, and should not extrapolate from a comment a general feeling or rank criticism and restoration in the same order. Microcases. Theory of the Gimmmick: Aesthetic Judgment and Capitalist Form* analyzes why the work appears to be both overworked and overstretched by both acclaimed and skeptical judgements; it is particularly appropriate to study AI writing, best-selling forms and cultural goods, because the judgement itself exposes conflicting expectations of labor, value and authenticity.
8.4.1 How emotions move, hinder and be named by daily aesthetics

Sara Ahmed's Emotional Cultural Politics (*The Cultural Policies of Equality*, 2nd ed.) analyzes how emotions circulate and “slubber” between words, bodies, objects and groups, emphasizing that emotions are not private objects that encapsulate individuals. (Ahmed, 2014) The Ugly Feerings* of Sianne Ngai focuses on low-intensity, non-discretionive negative emotions such as jealousy, irritation, anxiety and paranoia, which indicate how they can be formally reduced to impaired mobility; Our Aesthetic Scope: Odd, Cute, Interesting (* Our Aesthetic Matters: Zany, Cute, Interesting*) links Zany, Cute, Intesting to performance/production, consumption and information flows. (Ngai, 2005; 2012)

The three-tier mechanism cannot be combined into “the intensity of the emotional movement before the language”: Ahmad explains how emotions move along the symbol—physical relationships, how ugly emotions explain the disruption of action, and the daily aesthetic context shows how production-exchange-consumption enters the sense of judgement. Research can be used to codify the generic, action-oriented and aesthetic terms on this basis, avoiding only emotional-frequency statistics.
Chapter 9 - Cognitive literature, empirical aesthetics and neurology
9.1 Sources and frontier

The spectrum can be written as: Reaction/Psycho-Dictionary Studies, Cognitive Language and Cognitive Poetry, Experience Literature Studies, Neurocognitive Poetics, Forecast Processing, 4E Cognitive and Intercultural Acceptance. The Jacobs neurocognitive poetry model (NCPM) attempts to integrate familiarization with background, outlookization, insulation, aesthetic emotional and neurobehavioural indicators (Jacobs, 2015); Kukkonen uses prognosis to explain how readers form and modify probabilities about narratives (Kukkonen, 2020).

The real innovation of the 2020s was not “brain scanning of literature”, but a more sophisticated connection to the manageable text form - the reading process - the experience report - behavioural/physiological indicators. Hugentobler and Lüdtke manipulated semantic condensation by nine words, “micropoly”, and in 32 final samples, high-condensation projects were more likely to form uniform concepts, were considered more understandable and preferred; the study provided a design that separated background and outlook features (Hugentobler et al., 2021). Corcoran and others by poems/prosecutions x Whether or not the 2x2 design for end-line re-evaluation is required to validate the visual concept, 16 of the 27 persons forming complete data available; poetry conditions generally show more/more attention and look back, but the sample is small (Corcoran et al., 2023).

The intercultural orientation began to incorporate the perspective, insulation and cultural psychology of translation into cognitive translation (Chen et al., 2022). Giovanelli and the Cambridge Element of 2026 combine cognitive metaphors, cognitive grammar, text world with poetry/self-ethnic aspirations, showing that cognitive poetry is also developing into practice (Giovanelli et al., 2026).
9.2 Methodological tool chain

Concept: define “insinuation, stranger chemistry, literary, aesthetic, understanding, and common sense” separately; define text, subjective and behavioural indicators for each frame, avoiding a measurement.

Stimulant materials: Match length, frequency, metronology, subject matter, familiarity and layout; if a poem is rewritten as a essay, “poetry” and nature should be pre-tested, with all the incentives to be made public.

Samples and designs: ex ante efficacy analysis, exclusion criteria, random sequence, control conditions, language proficiency and literary experience; cross-language studies need to report bilingual background.
Measurement: different questions are answered by reading, eye motion, leather power, heart rate, EEG/fMRI; priority multilayer/mix effect model, with participants and text as random effects.

Explanation: A visible look at differences is not “deeper”; brain-based activation is not the same as a given aesthetic experience. The difficulty, frequency and mission needs are first eliminated before literary interpretation is offered.
Open Science: Pre-registration, irritation and analysis codes, de-marking data, effect and confidence interval, all analytical branches; small samples clearly marked “conceptual validation”.
9.3 How theory and experimentation are bound by each other

rows: Whether or not to slow down reading and enhance trials for prospectiveization Beautiful.
Original/minimal rewrite + Eye move + aesthetic/understanding + open association
Rewriting unnatural; difficult words, not prospection, cause slowdown
What happens when narrative predictions are broken?
Manipulate the order/turn of information + memory + probability judgement when reading online (Kukkonen, 2020)

Does the translation's perspective affect the insulation?
Same original multiple translations + bilingual samples + perspective label + insulation/rememination
Language proficiency, nature of translations, cultural familiarity (Chen et al., 2022)
Can creative practice produce cognitive knowledge?

Rationalization of self-reporting; not available for single author

blocks.text: Representation nodes: Jacobs/NCPM (Freie Universität Berlin tradition) (Jacobs, 2015); Hugentobler, Lüdtke ' s experiments intersect with neurocognitive psychology (Hugentobler et al., 2021); Vision of Liverpool team visual proof of concept (Corcoran et al., 2023); cultural neurocognitive discussion among Chinese multi-university translators (Chen et al., 2022); Cambridge Elements ' synthesis of cognitive philosophies (Giovanelli et al., 2026).

Application issues: perspective, metaphoric, narrative prediction, insulation, interlocution, poetry processing, translation acceptance. Limitations: WEIRD/student samples, small samples, irritation artificialization, mission effects, intercultural weight scale differentials, neuroreverse extrapolations, publication deviations. The most promising directions are multi-language cross-laboratory reproduction, natural reading of long text, behavior-depreciation hybrid models, rather than single “brain-area positioning”.
9.4 Theoretical mechanisms: Experiments can only be interpreted in a restrictive manner and cannot replace it.

Conceptual distinction. Cognitive literature research is conducted with attention, intellectual extrapolation, cognitive or predictive process of interpretation reading; empirically received studies to observe real readers by behaviour, eye movement, biology and interview; neuroaesthetics focus on neuro-related aesthetic experiences. The three sharing issues are at different levels of evidence: cognitive modelling mechanisms, experimental testing of local effects, and literary history explaining why stimulation is relevant for a given reader. Core mechanisms. Arthur M. Jacobs, in the “Neurocognitive Poetics: Methods and Models for Investmentalizing the Neuronal and Regulatory Bases of Legal Reception”, placed familiarization, visionization, immersion and aesthetic aesthetics in a multilayered process framework; Karin Kukkonen's *Probaility Designs: Literature and Probability Procising* viewed the work as “probability design”: the category and context formation of the text and the language, the expression, the perspective and the plot manufacturing prediction error, and the reader's understanding and aesthetic experience in updating the model. The literary form is thus not an incentive list but a historical device to control the expected rhythm. Most critical. Laboratory short text, average reaction and single-language samples may sacrifice long readings, rereadings, individual differences and cultural contexts; brain region-related stories can also easily be described as unproven causal stories. The border applies. The study should pre-registered the main assumptions, report sample, irritation, efficacy and negative results, and retain open memory and language validation; physical or eye indicators can only support local processing judgements. Microcases. Rhiannon Corcoran, Christophe de Bezenac and Philip Davis compare poetic and dissembling materials in retrospect mode in “`Looking Before and After': Can Simple Eye Tracking Patterns Distinguished Poetic from Prosaic Texts?”. The theoretical value of the design is not to prove that “poetry is deeper”, but to test whether particular forms change the course of attention; layout, length, difficulty and literary experience remain alternative interpretations that must be excluded.
Gender Research Frontiers: Cool, Transgender and the “anti-gender” Age
10.1 Spectrums: from performance to relationships, care and infrastructure

Butler's performance deconstructs the natural consistency of sex/gender/desiration, which is an important source of cool theory (Butler, 2006); however, the cross-gender study of the 2020s is not a simple application. The new directions include: BlackTrans feminism, transports of color, abolitionism, care infrastructure, mad/crip/trans intersection, climate of structural sexual violence, global urban identity, transgender Internet history and silence (Bey, 2022; Malatino, 2020; Awkward-Rich, 2022; Stanley, 2021; Aslı Zengin, 2024; Dame-Griff, 2023; Smilges, 2022).

Marquis Bey has shifted Black transfers feminism from a permanent representative to a fugitive, gender radicalism and power abolition awareness approach (Bey, 2022). Malatino extends “care” from intimate virtues to mutual aid, medical care, bureaucracy and exhausted infrastructure (Malatino, 2020). Awkward-Rich points out that the Trans research has been cut in conjunction with Madness, Billness and Disability to gain the legitimacy of the system, proposing “trans malays justice” to reread literary and theoretical archives (Awkward-Rich, 2022). Stanley's “Climate of Violence” extends the focus from individual hate crimes to manageable/ungoverable life-related structural hostility (Stanley, 2021).

Global and media orientation are equally critical. Zengin presented the “violence intimacy” of Istanbul's historical national identity, analysing how police, psychiatry, family, law, funerals and transkin together form the urban world (AslıZengin, 2024). Dame-Griff has been using digital archives and media archaeology to reconstruct the trans-gender Internet since the 1980s, proving the evolution of identity terms, communities and platform infrastructure (Dame-Griff, 2023). Smilges understood the silence of the Cools as a truism of action, and resisted setting the rhetoric and visibility as the only path to liberation (Smilges, 2022).
10.2 Frontline disputes

Identity category vs Analytical Operations: Trans is both a specific life/system category and can mean cross-cutting, transformation and relationship operations; the latter cannot eliminate the material violence to which the former is exposed (Bey, 2022; Aslı Zengin, 2024).

The double-edged sword of visible politics: archives and platforms can form a community and can trigger surveillance, cabinets, harassment and permanent preservation. Research on “discovering” historical pages does not automatically authorize the re-disclosure of personal data (Dame-Griff, 2023).

Care and regularization: Medical/family care supports life and may require a diagnostic and submissive subject; research needs to look at resources, labour and thresholds simultaneously (Malatino, 2020; AslıZengin, 2024).

Cool/cross-gender and disability: The “health, adaptation, integrity” criterion cannot be used as an emancipation criterion; the mad/crip perspective changes the understanding of narrative coherence, autonomy and repair (Awkward-Rich, 2022; Smilges, 2022).

Anti-identity politics dilutes responsibility: flight and de-genderization have theoretical power, but policies, violence data and community services still need to be in the temporary category. The strategic uses and injuries of the categories should be described simultaneously (Bey, 2022).
10.3 Methodological tool chain and ethics
Trans-of-color/cross-reading: analysis of race, class, handicap, colonization and gender without any axis as an “additional variable”.

Community archives and digital history: Wayback snapshot, BBS, mailing list, Zine and oral history cross-check; default minimum disclosure, vague and identifiable information, linking to the body of the remaining information (Dame-Griff, 2023).

National identity: Dynamic consent, pseudonym level, situational risk assessment, member verification and local language; avoiding making sex work, medical and violence details traceable files (AslıZengin, 2024).
(c) Maps of emotional/care: Record who provides emotional, information, transportation, housing, legal and medical labour, tracking depletion and resource flows (Malatino, 2020).
(c) Silence: denial of access, deletions, intermittent narratives are not considered missing data; analysis of silence as protection, rhetoric and non-translation (Smilges, 2022).
The history of the vocabulary: to preserve the historical self-proclaims and their context, avoiding retroactive correction in contemporary terms and the reproduction of humiliating classifications.

rows: Moving from incident database to atmosphere and system
Digital archives, media archaeology, platform governance

blocks.text: Application issues: Cross-gender literature, Cool Child Files, Platform Community, Care, Cities, Disability/Madical Health, Gender-based Voices. Limitations: English/US Theory Centre, sample “visited” bias, research cabinets, trauma consumption, generalization of non-diverse concepts into different linguistic cultures. High-quality research should make community risk, data access and the form of results as important as theory.
10.4 Theoretical mechanisms: moving from identity re-emergence to relationships, care and institutional environment

Conceptual distinction. Judith Butler *Gender Trouble: Feminism and the Subversion of Identity* say “activity” not by the person acting at will, but rather by the norm that, after repeated statements, physical practices and systems recognize the creation of seemingly stable genders. The Cool study examines how the norms of identity, time and desire fail; the cross-gender study deals more specifically with physical autonomy, medical care, law, labour, care and archives. Core mechanisms. An important shift in the 2020s has been from “the existence of certain categories of persons in the text” to “the infrastructure that makes certain lives sustainable”. Hil Malatino, in *Trans Care*, addresses care as a solidarity, emotional labour and resource allocation in crisis; Eric A. Stanley, *Atmospheres of Violence: Running Antagonism and the Trans/Queen Ungovernerable*, shows that violence is not just a set of isolated incidents, but also a climate of hostility that is sustained by law, media, space and expectations. Time interruptions, mismatches, silence and collective voices in literature can thus be considered as forms of organization of institutional experience. The strongest criticism. “All can be childized” dilutes specific physical and legal risks; the pursuit of visibility alone may increase surveillance, harassment and forced disclosure; and the direct transposition of English identity may also erase local naming history. The border applies. The study must retain author and community terminology, distinguish between national laws and the medical system, and establish hierarchical access and withdrawal for endangered Internet archives. Microcases. *The Two Reviews: A History of the Transgender Internet* treats the early network as both a site for the creation of identity words, mutual assistance and narrative forms, as well as a historical site for the takeover and loss of information from commercial platforms, and model literature studies on how to place personal writing, media interfaces, community governance and digital preservation in the same chain of interpretation.
10.4.1 “Anti-gender” is not a theoretical rebuttal, but rather a political assembly of fear

Judith Butler, Who's Fear of Gender? (* Who's Afrail of Gender?*), analyses transnational “anti-gender” mobilization as a political illusion: the idea that ginder is shaped to become a threatening target that can be brought together, magnify and divert economic anxiety, family anxiety, racism and power aspirations. (Butler, 2024) The purpose of the book is not to provide new genderist approaches, but to explain how the concept flows between religious organizations, political parties, social platforms, translation slogans and State policy. Case studies should record a spectrum of slogans, organizational funds, platform enlargement, legal texts and literature/cultural counter-narratives; national anti-gender movements cannot be considered the same reproduction, nor can social acceptance be inferred from text positions alone.
Chapter 11 Memory and trauma: multi-directional memory, associated subject and platform memory
11.1 Sources and new phases

The spectrum can be summarized as: collective/cultural memory trauma and witness post-mortemory multi-directional memory post-colonial, platformization and non-human memory agents. Hirsch ' s post-memory study examines how the next generation can establish strong relationships with uninvited trauma through family narratives, photographs and cultural forms (Hirsch, 2012). Rothberg opposed zero-sum competition between different victimization histories, suggesting that Holocaust and decolonization memories were borrowed and converted into public culture; then, the victim/sitter was compensated with the “involved subject” to analyse the responsibility of the perpetrator who benefited from the system and history but did not directly commit the violence (Rothberg, 2009; Rothberg, 2019).

The shift in the 2020s was to view memory as a technological-platform-political economic process. Keightley believes that post-colonial memory research cannot be a medium of transmission in analog/digital technology, but rather a study of infrastructure, ownership and the environment (Keightley, 2022). Smith, Jacobsen, Annabell summed up multiple memories, methods, worlds, subjects, dynamics, time and technology with "multipleness of platform-based memory" (Smit et al., 2024). Makortykh further distinguishes between the three types of memory transmission of people - humans, humans - robots, robots - and suggests search, referral, chat agency and generation systems as memory actors (Makhortykh, 2024).
11.2 New Arguments

Universalization of trauma paradigms: interpretation of colonization, war, migration as a possible shielding of local time, collective practice and continuing violence using Western clinical trauma models; need to be combined with post-colonial technological history (Keightley, 2022).

(c) Competing memory vs multi-way memory: cross-history comparisons can generate alliances or asymmetrical diversion; research seeks to track specific media, domains and power, rather than claiming “all wounds are the same” (Rothberg, 2009).

Visibility and the right to forget: Platform automatic alerts, recommendations and “supplied” photographs to change personal memory; permanent storage, deletion of failures and commercial sequencing to make forget a systemic issue (Smit et al., 2024).

Synthetic memory and AI agents: chat robots can retrieve, generate and even exchange past information between systems; the central question is not whether machines are human “rememberable”, but who sets values, corrects errors and respects the victim's obligation (Makhortykh, 2024).

The responsibility goes beyond direct victimization: literary and memorial research can be used to analyse “involved subjects” in the supply chain, settler inheritance, indirect location of viewers and platform users (Rothberg, 2019).
11.3 Methodological tool chain
Memory vector spectrometry: cross-family photographs of the same event, novels, memorials, textbooks, social platforms and AI response tracking; recording of each remedial deletion.
Multi-way comparison: establish physical links to the exchange/exhibit/activity/translation, re-analyze borrowing, competition, silence and alliances (Rothberg, 2009).

Oral history and post-rememberance: intergenerational interviews, photo presentations, family files; avoiding interviewing to force a full narrative of trauma, allowing silence and withdrawal (Hirsch, 2012).

Platform go-live: new account/historical account cross-check, recording recommendations, searches, automatic alarms, deletions and data downloads; date, region, language and personalized status of freeze.

AI Memory Audit: Cross Model/Language/Time Repeat of the same historical issue, recording sources, omissions, hallucinations, victim representation and refusals; human expert verification, not publishing hazard generation details (Makhortykh, 2024).
Infrastructure research: Platform companies, archives, cloud storage, content auditing and energy material co-analysis (Keightley, 2022; Smith, etc., 2024).
11.4 Representation Case and Borders

blocks.text: Application issues: War/colonial/disaster literature, intergenerational memory, memorials, digital heritage, social platforms, Ai history narratives. Limitations: synthesizing trauma, victimization, inducement to witness, black boxes on platforms, contamination files of synthetic content, re-transmission of hazardous materials. It is important to distinguish between the four layers of “history facts” of “memory performance” and “memory practice” of “platform”.
11.5 Theory Mechanism Deepening: Memory is not a repository, but a relationship that is constantly being restructured by the media

Conceptual distinction. The trauma study focuses on how the experience of violence returns with delay, fracture or physical symptoms; Marianne Hirsch, in *The General of Postmemory: Writer and Vision Culture After the Holocaust*, presents “post-rememberance” explaining how the latter generation can establish strong relationships with the uninvited past through photographs, family narratives and silence; Michael Rothberg's *Multidical Memoury: Remembering the Holocaust in the Age of Decoration* rejects the interpretation of different victimization histories as zero and competition for limited public attention. Core mechanisms. Memory is constantly remediating through novels, photographs, memorials, courses, platform sequencing and generating responses, and every transfer diminishes, connects and redistributes credibility. Rothberg, in *The Implicated Subject: Beyond Victims and Perpetrators*, further named the “involved subjects”: persons who have not committed direct violence but have benefited from or sustained their effects from historical structures, are held responsible for different kinds of criminal offences. Memory research thus moves from “who remembers what” to “who maintains what relationship through what device”. Most critical. If all history is put into the inexhaustible, flashback and healing scripts, the trauma paradigm will level the gap between colonization, war, chronic violence and daily oppression; moral memory research may also predetermine the right way to remember. The border applies. It is necessary to preserve asymmetries in events, languages, laws and power; platform data cannot represent the entire social memory; and war materials need to be minimized and re-identified. Microcases. Rik Smit, Benjamin Jacobsen and Taylor Annabell broke the platform memory into multiple combinations of data, algorithms, subject matter and time in “The Multimediaties of Platforming”, suggesting that an old post of “return” was neither a mere personal memory nor a one-sided manipulation of algorithms, but a temporary outcome of interface alerts, business sequencing, user action and public events.
11.5.1 Post-colonial correction of trauma theory: from single event to historical scale

The post-colonial witness: Crossing the Trauma: Trauma Out of Borders* system of Stefan Craps criticized the European centrality of the classic trauma theory, the single-disaster event model and narrow trauma aesthetics, and advanced the cross-traumatization nexus with colonial and post-colonial literature. (Craps, 2013) This is not a renaming of all suffering as trauma, but rather a time-based mechanism that requires a distinction between one-off events, structural violence, chronic poisoning, intergenerational deprivation and witness to war. Methodologically, it is possible to code the symptoms, historical archives, witness relationships and the system's continuity layer; it is more important to preserve legal status, colonial history and visibility differences and to prevent “trusion solidarity” from pacifying asymmetries once again.
Medical humanities, narrative medicine and disability research
12.1 From disease stories to critical medical humanities

Frank ' s " Wounded Talker " brings disease narratives, body and ethics into social theory (Frank, 2013); Charon ' s narrative medicine carries detail, attention, signs and association to clinical narratives (Charon, 2006). Two subsequent criticisms of the change: Woods noted that not all suffering could/would be described, and that “discussion” could become a normative and moral pressure (Woods, 2011); Viney, Callard, Woods presented critical medical humanities, demanding a struggle with life sciences, political economy, physical environment and institutional practices, rather than the co-decorative of humanities as doctors (Viney, et al., 2015). The Edinburgh Critical Medicine Humanist Guide systematizes this interdisciplinary structure (Whitehead et al., 2016).

New growth points in the 2020s include: healthy humanities, graphic medicine, handicapped/crip medical literature, global health narratives, care work, chronic diseases/long COVID, digital health and participatory public humanities. The Diedrich synthesis can be traced to the American Literature-Medical Spectrograph (Diedrich, 2023); the 10th edition of the Image Medicine Proclamation summing up the expansion of cartoons in caring, education, disability and marginal voices (Czerwiec et al., 2025); and Sterne combines “incapacitation” with media history, self-ethicidal and political phenomena in her own voice, hearing, fatigue, and considers “incomparable” as a universal but unequal political-physical dimension (Sterne, 2022). Cohen, from the medical “no idea” to reflect on the gap between healing and biomedical knowledge (Cohen, 2023).
12.2 Core contentions

Whether narrative good is over-suppositioned: coherence, rehabilitation, enlightenment is not the goal of all patients; debris, silence, repetition and endlessness are also of intellectual value (Woods, 2011).

Humanities are clinical tools or critical partners: if they are only justified by increasing the value of the same/satisfaction, they avoid hospital labour, insurance, race, disability and pharmaceutical power (Viney et al., 2015; Whitehead et al., 2016).

Diseases and disabilities are not the same: there are differences between the desire for treatment, identity, accessibility and anti-cure politics; research must hear the disabled person and not acquiesce in restoring “normal” (Stene, 2022).
Natural empowerment of images: comics can express time, pain and invisible symptoms, but publication, visual exposure and reader scrutiny can still simplify or consume pain (Czerwiec et al., 2025).

(b) Rehabilitation and healing: the improvement of medical technology is different from the meaning of life, the restoration of relationships; literature presents this distinction, but cannot substitute evidence of medical effects (Cohen, 2023).
12.3 Methodological tool chain
Narrative/Form Analysis: Time, Voice, Perspective, Inflection, Diagnosis, Silence, Discretion and Endurance; Avoid coding patient stories as disease “attitude”.

Narrative interviews: open questions, sequence and pause of participants, trauma-informed processes, separation of clinical and research roles; member verification does not force participants to accept the researcher ' s explanation.
Image/multi-modular analysis: grid frames, lines, colours, blank pages, text-image conflicts, body perspectives; combined with the creator process interview, as necessary (Czerwiec et al., 2025).

Clinical education research: Predefined results (observation, communication, diagnostic reasoning, bias), setting up control/pre- and post-natal measurements and long-term follow-up; not using only after-school satisfaction.
Participatory/co-creation: Patients, carers, health care and artists decide issues together, sign, privacy and public level.

Critical system analysis: integration of electronic patient template, insurance classification, hospital space, waiting time, nursing work and digital health platforms into materials (Viney et al., 2015; Whitehead et al., 2016).

Ethics and data: IRB/ethic review, minimization of medical identity, revocable, individual consent to the creation of an AI to deal with sensitive narratives; do not automatically consider publicly available disease blogs as risk-free data.

blocks.text: Application issues: Disease/care literature, clinical communication, cartoons, disability, mental health, public health narratives, digital health. Limitations: therapeutic overstretch, patient story-tooling, privacy, doctor-centred, universalization of the North American medical system, overextension of qualitative samples. Researchers should clearly separate literature interpretation, experience knowledge and evidence of clinical effects.
12.4 Theory Mechanism Deep Scratch: Why can't you listen to stories while they are at the same time heard?

Conceptual distinction. Narrative medicine organises close reading, writing and clinical relationships for care, reproduction and belonging; critical medical humanities ask how medical knowledge, systems, physical environment and body work together to produce “diseases” and “normal”; and disability research further distinguishes between impairment, social handicaps and physical-environmental relationships. Rita Charon's *Narrative Medicine: Honoring the Houses of Illness* provides a systematic framework for clinical narrative capability, but it is not a simple prescription for “reading novels and become one-minded”. Core mechanisms. The disease story forms pain, time, diagnosis and identity in communication, and is bound by a medical record template, insurance system, patient authority and acceptable outcome. William Viney, Felicity Callard and Angela Woods, in “Critical Medical Humanities: Embracing Environment, Taking Riskss”, “During against humanities” complements medicine only: literature, clinical, technical and social structures should be integrated into causal and accountability analysis. Most critical. Angela Woods warned in the “The Limiteds of Narrative: Productions for the Medical Humanities” that not all experiences can, should or should be organized into coherent stories; and that patients are asked to tell understandable and rehabilitative stories that may create new normative pressures. The border applies. Literary analysis does not prove the effectiveness of clinical treatment, nor does public reporting of diseases amount to consenting to unlimited reuse; research should retain silence, debris, refusal and co-signature. Microcases. * The Wooded Storyteller: Body, Illness, and Ethics, 2nd ed.* Distinguishing narratives such as recovery, disorder and claim, which are appropriate for comparing the time of disease with how it is formalized; their types should be used as the starting point for analysis, rather than as a fixed repulsion grid.
12.4.1 “Systeming” and the future of the handicapped: a unified analysis of relationships, environment and time

Rosemary Garland-Thomson defined “misfitting” in Misty: A Feminist Materialist Disability as a relationship between a specific body and a spatial-time environment, rather than a flaw within the individual. (Garland-Thomson, 2011) Alison Kafer, Feminist, Queer, Crip*, criticized the political/relationship model for compulsory soundness, linear rehabilitation time and pre-enclosed handicapped future, linking disability justice to the environment, reproduction and trans-gender politics. (Kafer, 2013)

Together, they move the unit from “damaged individuals” to a future configuration of the body-environment-system. Literary research can then compare thresholds, traffic, page formats, narrative rhythms, care work and how the future can be perceived to create participatory together; but relationship models cannot remove pain, fatigue and specific impairments, nor can they be used as a uniform interpretation of all disability experiences.
Sound, media and material texts: media archaeology and history of books
13.1 Theory spectrometry and new objects

The " Hearable Past " of Sterne rewriting " sound reproduction with medical, physical, industrial capital and sound technology, proposing a hearing technique (audiile technique) rather than natural hearing history (Sterne, 2003); MP3 further describes the format as a combination of sensory models, compressive standards, industrial infrastructure and hearing habits (Sterne, 2012). The media archaeology thus requires researchers not only to explain the content but also to track equipment, formats, interfaces, protocols and technical time (Ernst et al., 2012; Emerson, 2014).

The field then completed three decentrizations. First, Stoever's “sonic color line” and “listening ear” analyzes how race is created through listening norms (Stoever, 2016); Eidsheim rejects the natural attributes of the sound, emphasizing that sound is produced by both material, training, speech and listener (Eidsheim, 2019). Second, Redrawing the Global South Voice Research has shifted technology, space and perception from the history of audio recordings in Europe and the United States to many parts of the South (Steingo et al., 2019). Third, Robinson's “hungry listening” judges settlers who use indigenous voices as resources to raise relationships and reject listening ethics (Robinson, 2020).

New targets in the 2020s include: synthetic voice/speak assistant, voice books and podcasts, voice cloning, play sounds, sound and environmental sensing, hearing impairments, interface sounds and digital archives. Faber tracks how “computer sounds” are sexified, personalized and hidden from work from Star Trek to Siri (Faber 2020); Sterne's " Capturing " puts sound technology, silence, hearing and fatigue into political phenomena (Sterne, 2022).
13.2 Methodological tool chain

(c) The history of media substances: recording equipment models, carriers, formats, sampling rates, compression, play chain and preservation migration; the same document is not equally priced for different players/speakers (Stene, 2012; Ernst et al., 2012).
Archives listen: combine catalogues, audio tags, production of documents, censorship, radio forms and oral history; “no audio recordings” may come from technical, power or preservation deviations.

Acoustic analysis: waveform, spectrum, high sound, intensity and rhythm can describe sound, but not based on a single spectra to judge race, sex or emotion; it needs to be combined with performance history and listening to national aspirations (Eidsheim, 2019).
Listen to national aspirations/speaks: participation in observation, sound journals, audio recordings, interviews; explicit voice consent, bystander privacy, geographical sensitivity and context.

Interface forensics: Record menu, default sound, voice error, recommendations, access options and updated version; screenshot/record and audio Hash to create a reviewable snapshot (Emerson, 2014; Faber, 2020).

Repetition and restoration: Repetition of old equipment/formats should distinguish between original carriers, simulations, repair and speculation; recording of every step of noise reduction/balance, and preservation of unprocessed versions.
Relationship Listening: Researchers explain how they are trained to listen, who has the right to play/relay, and which voices should remain closed (Robinson, 2020).
13.3 Representational Results, Cases and Constraints

blocks.text: Questions of application: Sound literature, radio drama, poetry reading, podcasts, synthetic voice, archival recordings, electronic literature, and the ethnicization of hearing. Limitations: file silence, unavailability of equipment, restoration of hypocrisy, acoustic decision theory, unconscioned recording, sound extraction from body/place. The strongest design is the four-tier sync of the "technology chain-hearing system-text/form-specific listeners".
13.4 Theoretical mechanisms in depth: sound properties are often created by listening systems together

Conceptual distinction. Sound research studies examine hearing techniques, sensory training and voice politics; voice studies focus on body, identity and sound; media archaeological tracking devices, formats, interfaces and technical time; material text studies incorporate paper, documents, codes, servers and history of damage. The “sound” of literature is therefore neither a pure metaphor nor a natural attribute of sound waves. Core mechanisms. Jonathan Sterne explained in *The Audiblée Past: Cultural Organisms of Sound Reproduction* that modern hearing is first trained in listening, communication and recording practice; its *MP3: The Meeting of a Format* further reveals that compressed formats include sounding assumptions about hearing and unhearable psychoacoustics in technical standards. The format is not a neutral container but a pre-selected detail that is worth saving. *The Sonic Color Line: Race and the Cultural Policies of Leadership* and Nina Sun Eidsheim *The Race of Sound: Listing, Timbre, and Vocality in African American Music* interprets “hearing race” as a biological fact that is classified by listeners trained in history, not by voice. Most critical. If visual centerism is simply replaced by sound theory, sound research will re-enact the essence; digital recordings may also extract restricted, ritual or community voices again. The border applies. The analysis of audio, synthetic sound or oral files shall be accompanied by the recording of performers, equipment, clippings, formats, platforms and authorizations, respecting the right to be heard, not to be heard and not to be held in public or in local custody. Microcases. *Hungry Living: Resonant Theory for Indigenous Sound Studies* describes colonial listening as “hunger” in pursuit of possession and understanding, and suggests relationship listening that is not aimed at full readability, providing a complete correction of the literary sound archives from collecting ethics to interpreting borders.
##13.4.1 From Clipbook to Open Digital Book: The integrity of the work has also a media history

Whitney Trettien, Cutting/Copy/Playing: A Story of Bookmaking (*Cut/Copy/Paste: Fragments from the History of Bookwork*), published in early modern scrapping, collaborative compilation, page migration and open digital publishing, contradicting the use of “one author - complete work - linear printing history” as a natural form of books. (Trettien, 2021) The Minnesota University Press Manifold Open Edition is not an auxiliary mirror of paper content, but transforms searchable materials, images and reading paths into a demonstration infrastructure. The study of “works” should thus be accompanied by a record of how pages are slashed, reorganized, bound, digitized and re-linked; the debris is not a flaw waiting for recovery, but may also be the basic operation for book production.
The second part of the article, the eight cutting edges of literature research.

Eleven of the topics in this report are mainly to answer the literature study “what is being studied”; but without tracking how the work is created, coded, published, recommended, translated, taught, preserved and withdrawn, the subject is still mistook for being a stable text that automatically reaches the reader. The remarkable changes of recent years are that production, circulation, law, education and preservation are no longer just external contexts but are part of the effectiveness of literary forms and knowledge. The following eight cutting-edges are not separate and new and separate subjects, but rather serve as a second level of analysis that cuts across digital humanities, world literature, gender, environment, medicine, voice and regional research.
X.1 Text to public consequences: a complete research chain

Literary objects can be re-presented along the following chains: creation and collaboration of a version, code and rights attribution, choice of label, recommendation and reader work, translation and migration, teaching, archives and public use, community governance, maintenance and withdrawal. Each link in the chain should ask four questions: who has the right to decide and interpret; which jobs are paid, signed or absorbed free of charge; how laws, cultural agreements and technological infrastructure can be changed to researchable materials; and whether the chain of evidence remains in place after project liquidation, platform closure or community withdrawal of permission.
