Case SA-B: DREAMSEA - Digital preservation is also a ownership politics

The DREAMSEA field cover paper, palm leaves, bark and multilingual Buddhist/Islamic manuscripts, with the term “receiving location/holder” being retained as a context. The scale of the first phase and the second phase of 2026 indicate that the project is of a continuing nature. (DREAMSEA, n.d.; PPIM UIN Jakarta, 2026) The official “Knowledge Activism” paper further noted that digital donations cannot replace the participation of holders. (Shofiyanti et al., 2026) The main report compares it with ACO, ALMEDA: Who decides the metadata matrix? Where's the server? How are the rights in original/image/relay distributed?
Case SA-C: Coole of the Philippines goes to colonial literature class

Garcia's 2024 paper viewed nationalism as both an anti-colonial resource and a possible framework for repressing/distinguishing differences; the classroom approach was thus read in parallel with classic criticism, local vocabulary and contemporary coolies. (Garcia, 2024) A poetry competition in Busilak offers teaching local language. The common denominator with Hong Kong unruly Comparison is the rejection of a single modern time line; the difference is that the Philippine case is more directly involved in the curriculum than in the national culture.
18.5 Arguments and asymmetries

English still controls “visible South Asia studies”. The Shahitya Akademi English translation mechanism has expanded cross-lingual readability and may also make English the necessary intermediary for the encounter of languages. (Sahitya Akademi, n.d.)

India's size effect has overshadowed neighbouring countries. India has national institutes, ITT and large digital projects; local journals and non-English-language results in Pakistan, Bangladesh, Nepal, Sri Lanka are significantly weaker in international access. Tariq Rahman's literature dated back to 1988, and is a structural updating gap. (Rahman, 2015)

Digital preservation may repeat “scrap-North” hosting. DREAMSEA has UIN/PPIM co-governance advantages, but technology, funding and server cooperation continue to cross German/United States institutions; long-term access, data migration and local copies need to be included in the evaluation. (DREAMSEA, n.d.)

Caste and gender cannot be replaced. The work of Rege shows that pan-Indian feminism conceals the intellectual position of Dalit women; similar warnings apply to tribal/indigenous and north-eastern language literature. (Rege, 2006; Sahitya Akademi, 2024)

The internationalization of periodicals may again be English. Humaniora has been a reference point in English since 2022, but it may also raise the threshold for local authors. (Faculty of Cultural Science, Universal Gadjah Mada, 2025)
Latin America: Extractive, Platform and Decolonization Practices
19.1 Academic ecology and hotspot sequencing

The “latest” of Latin American literature research cannot be judged by the growth of English-language keywords alone. The region ' s achievements are numerous in Spanish, Portuguese, open warehousing, collective projects and public platforms, often with innovations that have been created to targets that were not present before or that could disappear at any time. Red de Humanidades Digitales (RedHD), established in 2011, organizes ongoing training, meetings and exchange of methods, which explains that digital humanities are not a one-way input of Northern tools, but have their own networks and normative discussions. (RedHD, n.d.) The Digital Atlas of Latin America of the Carolina Gainza team in Chile and the Digital Warehousing of the Rejane Rocha team in Brazil have transformed the identification, preservation, classification and presentation of the work itself into a literary historical argument. (Gainza et al., n.d.; Rocha, 2022)

The second structural feature is decolonization, ecology and the physical interlocking of media. Mignolo and Walsh's “concept-analysis-practice” chain, Escobar's “pluriversal politics” and Gómez-Barris' “inundated perspective” suggest approaches from knowledge, autonomy/design and extraction areas, rather than simply using post-colonialism as the subject of text. The third feature is material criticism of the world literary system: Locane tracks publishing houses, agents, translators, financial and aesthetic formats to produce a “Latin American literature” as a global commodity in circulation; and the study of world literature in Central America resists replacing the region with Mexico, Argentina, Brazil and “magic realism”. (Locane, 2019; Sophie Esch (ed. ), 2023)

rows: To colonize, to diversify the universe and to global South knowledge politics.

Theory monographs, digital literature criticism, ecological/indigenous research are mutually supportive; the core is knowledge-producing relationships, not the addition of “Southern text” (Kozak, 2023; Mignolo et al., 2018; Escobar, 2020; Gómez-Barris, 2017)
Digital/electronic literature, critical archives and media materiality

Regional networks, maps, Brazilian warehousing, monographs and several peer-reviewed papers constitute complete ecology (RedHD, n.d.; Gainza et al., n.d.; Gainza, 2018; Rocha, 2022; Kozak, 2023; Kozak, 2024)
Environmental Humanities, Extractism and Indigenous Literature

“By body-territory” after “scave” intersects with the health/indigenous narrative, and studies from natural appearances to resource systems and survival worlds (Escobar, 2020; Gómez-Barris, 2017; Gago, 2020; Andermann, 2018; García, 2023)
Feminism strikes/internationalism links physical violence, debt, care and territory to transnational analysis; and access to memory and digital platforms (Gago, 2020; Jelin, 2021)
World literature, translation and publishing sociology
From text “worldwide” to material conditions, value chains, gatekeepers and intraregional disparities; significant expansion of Central American cases (Locane, 2019; Sophie Esch (ed. ), 2023)
Memory, trauma, human rights and intergenerational relations
Timeline network from authoritarian/State violence memory to family, gender, social movements and rights systems (Jelin, 2021)
AI-Mundo, Technical Poemology and Critical Experimentism
Outcome 2025 proposes AI-Mundo, which does not automatically decentrize post-human/intellectual equivalence, but requires analysis of algorithms, platforms and colonial data (Kozak, 2025)
As a cross-front, challenges Western medical literature to narrow the narrative of medical and medical patients only (García, 2023)
There are fewer independent “facilitative theory” projects in this evidence pool, often embedded in feminism, memory, violence and territorial research
There is a potential for digital sound and media materiality, but this is not enough nuclei to describe the region as high heat; this is a search/visibility judgement, not “no existence”.

blocks.text: 19.2 Representing scholars-institutions-results

rows: Regional network for Latin America launched by Mexico
Building decentralized digital humanistic practices with common training, project exchange and regulation; the network itself is a regional methodological infrastructure
Universidad Diego Portales，Laboratorio de Literatura Digital
Map of Latin American Digital Literature; Narrativas y poéticas digitales (Gainza et al., n.d.; Gainza, 2018)
Putting electronic literature into information capitalism, platforms and regional preservation issues, rather than just doing novelty studies
The Brazilian objects are structured using databases, classifications and digital literature maps; emphasis is placed on “collecting/maintenance of the literary history”
Argentine network, such as CONICET / Universalidad de Buenos Aires
The colonial infrastructure of digital literature is criticized in the “Souths” plural, material, anarchic archives (anarchivism) and AI-Mundo.
Trans-American Decolonization Network; Walsh long-standing in the Andean region
Distinction of concepts, analysis and practice, emphasizing that decolonization requires changes in intellectual relations and ways of acting
Colombian Thought/Action Network and Inter-American Institutions
Designing for “A world that accommodates many worlds”, autonomy and transition against a single modernity
Latin American/North American cross-regional cultural studies
Resisting the state/business extraction of viewing with an “inundation perspective” in art, field and social movements
UBA / CONICET and Ni Una Menos Network
Extending feminism from identity issues to strikes, debt, care, violence and physical-territorial politics
Latin American Literature - European Publications Research Cross-Regional Nodes
Explain what Latino literature can become “world literature” by its version, publisher, agent and value chain
Transnational Research Network on Central American Literature
Central American Literatures as World Literature （Sophie Esch (ed.)，2023）
Bringing war, migration, indigenous/African experience, translation and small publishing markets into world literature
Latin American Cultural/Environmental Humanist Transregional Network
From landscape to landscape-based art-natural relations and ecological crises
Connect families, gender, memory, human rights and social movements to avoid the trauma being written as a psychological state only
Universitat de València, study on First Nations literature in Latin America
Evidence that health narratives, ecological destruction and indigenous cosmology must be analysed jointly and that regional knowledge flows within European institutions are being informed

blocks.text: 19.3 Approach shift
19.3.1 From "digitalization of classics" to "suspension of digital originals"

Digital protobiography relies on Flash, browser plugins, social platforms, servers and author accounts, and works may disappear as a result of technology phase-out or commercial shutdowns. The Gainza Map Project identifies and preserves works before analysing narratives and poetry; Rocha's Brazilian warehouse processes classifications, metadata and displays simultaneously. The files here are not neutral containers: what is "Brazilian digital literature", whether social media writing is included, and how interactive versions are recorded, are producing literary history.

Recommended protocols: preservation of source files, operating environment videos, reader interactive paths, code/platform versions and author interviews; recording of invalid components rather than falsifying “complete recovery”; obtaining author and community authorization; setting up access levels for vulnerable or politically sensitive works; and placing maintenance costs and platform reliance on writing-in-writing methods.
19.3.2 From universal “digital” to Southern materiality

Kozak rejects a homogeneous global South with a “Souths” plural and re-incorporates linguistic hegemony, material-technical conditions and institutional resource inequalities into digital literature analysis. (Kozak, 2023) Her “anarchivism” does not lack files, but questions their ownership, classification and colonial data order. (Kozak, 2024) The study of electronic texts therefore cannot be a screen reading, but also tracks the infrastructure, platform conditions and maintenance work on which the works depend.
19.3.3 Device criticism from "AI Generate New Texts" to "AI-Mundo"

The Al-Mundo of Kozak 2025 sees AI as a worldwide device, not a single tool, for infiltration into language, perception, education, art and governance; the aesthetics of the so-called “go to the centre of humanity” may mask the geology, data and material conditions of hegemonic digital devices. (Kozak, 2025) The “critical experimentalism” she proposed allowed artists to use models but required that their works be exposed, interfered with or re-engineered. Methodologically, the hint/output, model/date, editing process, and the known boundaries of data and sources of computation should be preserved; the latter two are audit requirements that are presented in this report and do not pretend to be the full factual conclusions of the text.
19.3.4 From post-colonial texts to decolonization practices

Mignolo/Walsh divides decolonization into concepts, analyses and prachis, reminding researchers that “citing Southern theories” is not tantamount to changing knowledge relationships. (Mignolo et al., 2018) The pluriverse of Escobar uses autonomous communities, territorial struggles and design practices as knowledge fields; the submerged lifestyle of Gómez-Barris looks for perception and life styles that are being pushed from the State/capital perspective in the extraction area. The methods of enforcement include joint decision-making, payment of community research work, recognition of collective authors, and the refusal to disclose and give feedback to local language results.
19.3.5. From landscape map extraction to extraction mechanisms and body territory

The “post-pastic” of Andermann states that traditional landscapes organize nature as a visible and accessible national space, while contemporary art and literature expose ecosystems, infrastructure and non-human actors. (Andermann, 2018) Gago's body-territory connections to gender-based violence, care, debt and resource competition; Martínez García combines medical, environmental and indigenous literature, suggesting that the classification of pollution, disease and colonial medicine is often the same narrative structure. (Gago, 2020; García, 2023)
19.3.6 Material society from “global dissemination success” to the publishing chain Learn.

Locane distinguishes local/small-scale publishing forms experiments from the translation, marketing and marketing formats preferred by the multinational publishing community, tracking how editors, agents, sponsors, festivals and translators produce “worldwide”. (Locane, 2019) The Central American collection of papers shows that countries, languages, ethnic groups and war experiences within the region should not be represented by a small number of international personalities. (Sphie Esch (ed. ), 2023) Research in world literature should establish a network of product-version systems and institutions, rather than merely comparing themes.
19.4 Three deep case studies
Case A: From Maps to Storage - Object-building in Latin American Digital Literature

Gainza 2018 has placed Latin American digital narratives in information capitalism, emphasizing interfaces, codes, participation and platform conditions; the successor map project has transformed scattered, obsolete and uncalculated works into searchable regional objects. The Brazilian digital literature warehouse of UFScar shows that Portuguese traditions cannot be merely an appendix to the Spanish “Latin America”. (Rocha, 2022)

A high-quality comparison project can be used to create a bilingual version of 30 works from Chile/Argentina Spanish and Portuguese, each of which is based on "local operation/platform dependence, code openness, mode of interaction, language, version survival, author self-positioning"; then to cross-reference works that cannot be classified. Results should include sustainable snapshots, data dictionary, loss list and author advice. If only screen maps or text are taken, the interaction rhythm, sound and code behavior is miswritten into traditional page literature.
Case B: AI-Mundo - An AI literary study on how to do “critical but still experimental”

The Kozak problem is not to reject AI, but to naturally transform the generation model into an autonomous, locationless creator. Her previous studies on Souths, materiality and anarchivism constituted a continuous approach: first, asking what geo-data order the digital device was based on, then looking at how the work was moving away from that order. (Kozak, 2023; Kozak, 2024)

Case design is available for two projects of the same Latin American digital poet using closed-source large models and local small models: recording language support, review, tips, failure output, manual modification, time/cost; inviting authors to explain why they retained errors; comparing Spanish, Portuguese and indigenous terminology treatment; and blind readers who do not use “man-like” as the only criterion for success. The key result may not be text quality, but how the work reveals the model ' s preconceived language, subject and world.
Case C: Extracting Area - Multiverse - Aboriginal Health Narrative

Gómez-Barris looked for the social ecology of mining, infrastructure and cultural works, which was being pushed from a sexual perspective; Escobar emphasized that the Territory was not a resource container, but a world in which it was connected. A literary study by Martínez García shows that diseases, rivers, plants and colonial violence in First Nations narratives cannot be classified separately as “medicine” or “environment”. (García, 2023)

Replicable research should include a combination of novels/poetry, environmental change records, oral history and community knowledge consultations. Researchers cannot use the indigenous cosmology as an example of Western theory alone, nor should they disclose the geographical knowledge that is sacred or potentially exploited by businesses. The unit could be “relationship events” — how a river pollution changes the body, relatives, species, language and rituals simultaneously — rather than the frequency of pre-separated subject matter.
19.5 Themes map

rows: Regional networks, digital protobiography maps, Brazilian warehousing and archives critical; infrastructure building itself is theory (RedHD, n.d.; Gainza et al., n.d.; Rocha, 2022; Kozak, 2024)
From concept to pragsis, puriverse, Suths and extraction areas; against homogenous data sources in the South (Kozak, 2023; Mignolo et al., 2018; Escobar, 2020; Gómez-Barris, 2017)

The core sources of the subregion did not form a verifiable high-density cluster; subsequent access to local periodicals of experimental aesthetics, psycho-neurological literature in Brazil should be provided
Feminism International, Body Territory and Memory Research; Insufficient coverage of cross-gender literature and Caribbean Cool Studies in this evidence pool (Gago 2020; Jelin, 2021)
Regional characteristics of extractionism, landscapes, autonomous worlds and healthy indigenous ecosystems (Escobar, 2020; Gómez-Barris, 2017; Andermann, 2018; García, 2023)
The publication of sociological and decentrized Central American revisions to the Universal Literature, which is only about transnational themes/classics (Locane, 2019; Sophie Esch (ed. ), 2023)
AI-Mundo, critical experimentalism and Southern material composition are different theoretical pathways from the “model capability competition” (Kozak, 2023; Kozak, 2025)

Digital literature preservation necessarily involves sound and invalid media, but this time there is not enough regional peer review clusters; this should not be the basis for determination of inactivity (Gainza et al., n.d.; Gainza, 2018)
Crossing of environmental destruction, indigenous literature and colonial knowledge, rather than copying British-American narrative medicine (García, 2023)
Many are embedded in feminism, mobilization, memory, violence and territorial relations; need to complement the theory of independent dynamics and literature to be studied (Gago 2020; Jelin, 2021)
The trauma of dictatorship extends to the family, gender, human rights, social movements and multiple times (Jelin, 2021)

blocks.text: 19.6 Arguments, blind spots and research opportunities

The institutional paradox of decolonization: English-North Press helps to spread concepts around the world and may also make decolonization a high-value academic brand. The report should record the theoretical origin, field/movement relationship, place of publication and the proceeds of reference respectively. (Mignolo et al., 2018; Escobar, 2020)

Archives and extraction: Digital preservation may bring social works into unauthorized reuse or model training. Openness should not be tacitly accepted as unlimited download; “is available” and “is available for extraction” need to be separated. (Rocha, 2022; Kozak, 2024)

The digital divide is wrong: writing down low bandwidth/old equipment as being backward would ignore the strategic use of limited technology by artists; but romantic want would also mask inequality. (Kozak, 2023)

Internal disparities within “Latin America”: the evidence of the draft is clearly biased towards the publication of Argentina, Chile, Brazil and transnational English; Andean, Caribbean, Central America, Haiti/French-speaking Community, First Nations language coverage is still inadequate. The study of the world literature in Central America is only the starting point for correction. (Sofie Esch (ed. ), 2023)

World literary value chains: the visibility of international awards/translations cannot represent regional influence; local small publishing houses, digital dissemination, schools and oral networks should be studied simultaneously. (Locane, 2019)

(a) Categorization of health and ecology: The direct encodement of indigenous text using the British-American model of illness narrativity can be a metaphor for relationships to sexual health. The classification of studies must be developed in conjunction with language/community experts. (García, 2023)

Research opportunities: establishment of the Caribbean-Central America-Andean Digital Literature Joint Preservation Project; comparison of multinational group versions with local editions of redaction, cover, translator and marketing; tracking of changes in female slogans in poetry, street performance, social platforms and translation booths; establishment of a “non-extractive open archives” licence model; comparison of larger models for the memory, rejection and style integration of mixed Spanish, Portuguese and indigenous texts.
Africa: Water Theory, Decolonisation Metadata and Multilingual Network
20.1 Hot spots and academic ecology

The colonial study goes from “content representation” to language, metadata, home body and server. The language politics of the Ngolk, which is still a trend, has been transformed into a publishing mechanism by the pan-African translation practice of Jalala; and ALMEDA has gone further by using a multilingual Wikibase to reflect on why colonial library standards make small publications, oral, performances and unofficial publications invisible. (Jalada Africa, 2020; ALMEDA Research Project, n.d.; Anangenendt et al., 2025)

Southern Africa has produced a global water/ocean theory with greater global impact. Hydrocolonism of Isabel Hofmeyr links land and sea, empire, environment, customs, copyright and censorship; rewriting for water is a literary area with rain, rivers, sewers, groundwater, marine distress and water privatization. (Hofmeyr, 2019; Hofmeyr, 2022; Hofmeyr et al., 2024; Hofmeyr et al., 2022)

The environment is being transformed from an “ecological theme” to a physical, infrastructural, extraction and care environment. The Planetary Hinterlands of Sarah Nuttall and others studied the colonial heritage, capital extraction, abandonment and more-than-human care; Brutalism of Achille Mbembe linked capital, digital technology, human deformation and planetary destruction. (Gupta et al., 2024; Mbembe, 2024)

AI Relationship with oral/sound is an urgent issue. The 2024 South African Folklore Society, sponsored by UCT, directly focused on the theme “Oral freedom and the experts and the effects of AI”; and DHASA 2025, on the theme “The role of humanities in digital and AI”, illustrated that the focus of the discussion was not following models but rather the right to information, language resources and human judgement. (Digital Humanities Association of Southern Africa, 2025; Southern African Folklore Society, 2024)

The African feminism and theoretical criticisms continue to revise global gender theory. The Decolonization and Afro-Feminism of Sylvia Tamale colonizes colonial history, law, body, sex and knowledge systems; the reference of Oyèrónk' Oyěwmí is a foundational text that questions the universal applicability of the dualist/woman category of Western bodies. (Tamale, 2020; Oyèrónk Ma'Oyquim, 1997)

Animated materialism/ Trans-Victorism is an important African source of post-human research. Harry Garuba's 2003 animist materiaism is not a romantic tradition, but rather explains how the gods, modern technologies, goods and politics share the power of African culture; their path meets with the new materiaism and should not be absorbed by the latter. (Garuba, 2003)

The public infrastructure of literature emphasizes multilingual openness and informal literature. African Storybook provides open-access multilingual stories; Jalalada organises trans-African translation in Ngolg, Wole Soyinka; and ALMEDA creates linked metadata for poetry, oral, festival materials that are not recorded in the library. (Jalada Africa 2020; African Storybook, n.d.; ALMEDA Research Project, n.d.)
20.2 Representing scholars-institutions-results

rows: The material history and the marine environment are combined through customs, ports, copyright, review and book voyages. (Hofmeyr, 2019; Hofmeyr, 2022)
Charne Lavery, Sarah Nuttall and Hofmeyr
“Reaking for Water” (2022, DOI); and Methodological Book under the same name (2024)
Provide a teaching-able methodological toolbox: vertical/horizontal/vertical tracking of different water worlds and water knowledge. (Hofmeyr et al., 2024; Hofmeyr et al., 2022)
To extract, discard and care across the urban/natural dichotomy, linking literary imagination to planetary material politics. (Gupta et al., 2024)
The blog has been published in the context of the International Conference on Indigenous Peoples, which has been held in Cairo in December 2008. (Tamale, 2020)
The basic challenge to the universalisation of the Western gender dual should be applied in parallel with local fields and subsequent criticism. ♪ I'm not a fool ♪
The human boom that preceded the present era has brought forth the coexistence of African spiritual-technological-commodity objects in modernity. (Garuba, 2003)
The capital/technology shape of planets and bodies, linking AI, post-human and ecological crises, is described as “grainism”. (Mbembe, 2024)
A Death Retold in Truth and Rumour（2015）
Death narrative studies with official documents, media, hearsay and transnational powers are appropriate for memory/emotional/media methods. (Musila, 2015)
Correcting the colonial catalogue standard in a mobile, multilingual way; turning metadata itself into a literary theoretical problem. (ALMEDA Research Project, n.d.; Anangenendt et al., 2025)
Moses Kilolo, Wanjeri Gakuru and Jalada community
The translation of African languages into literature through the multilingual equivalents does not require the English-language world literary market to be followed. (Jalada Africa 2020)
Institutionalization of AI, humanities, digital language resources and local training; South Africa continues to be a visible regional hub. (Digital Humanities Association of South Africa, 2025)

blocks.text: 20.3 New theory/method

Water colonialism (hydrocolonism): Colonial power not only occupies land but also controls waters and texts through ports, customs, shipping routes, quarantine, dumping and copyright. (Hofmeyr, 2019; Hofmeyr, 2022)

Reading for water: tracing water sensory, political and dynamic; literary areas can be defined by watersheds, coasts, monsoons and water knowledge rather than by the State. (Hofmeyr et al., 2024; Hofmeyr et al., 2022)
Planetary hiinterlands: interpret seemingly marginal/released land as a condition for a global extraction system while looking for human and inhuman care practices. (Gupta et al., 2024)
Panimist materialism: Rejecting the “traditional spirituality/modern technology” dichotomy, the power of the confluence of objects, gods, goods, machines and politics. (Garuba, 2003)

Afro-feminist decognization: the seemingly neutral scope of historical “gender, family, human rights, law” and the distinction between the effects of colonialism on the different gender/class/community. (Tamale, 2020; Oyèrónk Ma'Oyquim, 1997)

(c) To colonize metadata/mobile: to annex the concept of African without English; to allow performances, orals, small publications, festivals and editions to have their own structure of description. (Aangenendt et al., 2025)

Oral - AI Audit: Address voice consent, community control, dialectal error recognition and voice personality rights before voice recognition, transliteration, translation and generation. (Southern African Folklore Society et al., 2024)
20.4 Case study
Case AF-A: Retaking for Water - from subject to elemental approach

2022 Specialized publications/papers propose reading “under waterlines, along infrastructure, between multiple water spirits”; 2024 methodology books organize texts such as those from South Africa, the Caribbean, as chapters on rain, water pipes, sewage, seawrecks, black water hysteria and water privatization. The innovation is to make water both material and intellectual, rather than merely symbolic. Research can be superseded with GIS, infrastructure archives, climate data and fine reading, but only rhetorical decorations of natural scientific data must be avoided.
Case AF-B: ALMEDA - Directory is not a neutral packaging

ALMEDA found that the standard of official libraries tends to include ISBN monographs and stable authors, unofficial poetry, oral, festivals and the publication of African languages, and thus disappears from the search. The team chooses to be independent Wikibase instead of directly subject to Wikidata's existing headings and creates a multilingual, linked mobile body. This makes the “missing of metadata” evidence of colonial knowledge history. The risk lies in the fact that the project is dominated by European funds and Uppsala; the true decolonization depends on the sustainability of the African partners in deciding on the categories, hosting and correcting.
Case AF-C: Language practice from Decolonisation Mind to Jalada/Storybook

Jalada not only discusses writing in the mother tongue, but organizes the same story to be published in a cross-African language; African Storybook supports children ' s multilingual reading in open licences, graphics/audio and remixable formats. (Jalada Africa 2020; African Storybook, n.d.) Both transform “language politics” into a production/distribution mechanism and also expose translation asymmetries: which languages have editors, formal linguists, fonts and digital tools and which can only be trans-redacted in English. The research indicators should not be limited to a few languages, but should also examine the signature of the interpreter, community use, return flow and the sustainability of the server.
20.5 Arguments and asymmetries

South Africa is overvisible. The English pages and infrastructure of Wits, UCT, UWC, DHASA have enabled Southern Africa to be much better searcher than French-speaking West Africa, Portuguese-speaking Africa, Central Africa and the Horn of Africa; this is a superseding of real ecological differences and search deviations. (Digital Humanities Association of Southern Africa, 2025; Southern African Folklore Society, 2024)

The colonization project remains dependent on Northern funds/honoring. The international cooperation capacity of ALMEDA, African Storybook has expanded, but whether governance, servers and occupational gains are being settled locally in Africa requires an audit. (Africa Storybook, n.d.; ALMEDA Research Project, n.d.)

The African language cannot be integrated. The formality, publishing market, national status and digital resources varied greatly; multilingual projects should report by language rather than replacing quality analysis with “57/100+ languages”. (Jalada Africa, 2020; African Storybook, n.d.)

There is tension between Afropolitan/entanglement and decolonization. Transnational movements, multiplicity and urban modernity can oppose mono-ethnicism, but they can also obscure class, rural and linguistic inequalities. Nuttall's “touching” should be read in parallel with Tamale/Oyquiwù, rather than in the same context. (Tamale, 2020; Oyèrónk Ma'Oyquim, 1997)

The risks of pan-phantial/indigenous knowledge academic repackaging. Garuba's theory is clearly modern and critical and cannot be reduced to ecologicalism of “African harmony with nature”. (Garuba, 2003)
Chapter 21 Middle East and Arab world: Computable classics, audio archives and testimony
21.1 Hot spots and academic ecology

The core of the Arabic digital humanities has moved from “scans” to computational text, text reuse and open versions. Arabian Collactions Online (ACO) provides Arabic books in the public domain; OpenITI provides source, metadata, sustainable citation and open standards; KITAB detects text re-use on OpenITI with passim and publishes 10GB-level open matching data. (New York University Abu Dhabi Library, n.d.; KITAB Project, n.d.; Smith et al., 2024; Nigst et al., 2025) Library of Arabian Literacity (LAL) links the version to the world literature through authoritative Arabic versions, English translations and free Arabic PDF. (Kennedy et al., n.d.)

The feminist archives and oral history are one of the most original literary and cultural research infrastructures in Egypt. The General, National, and the Arab Novel, of Hoda Elsadda, have developed a common analysis of masculinity, femininity, state and classical culture; Women and Memoory Forum (WMF) has continued to build oral files of female pioneers, actors, publishers and health advocates. (Elsadda, 2012; Elsadda, n.d.; Women and Memoory Forum, n.d.)

Palestinian research is driven from memory/trauma to witness, imprisonment, digital micro-story and “writing as hope”. The Plasalist Memories of Birzeit link individual/collective memory with in-depth visits, representative surveys and creative writing; Basil Farraj, a political subject in detention from hunger protest research; and Saleh Masharqa, analysing 336 Gaza youth videos from 2023-10-2025-01, using humor, sound, clipping, platform interaction and micro-story as cultural resistance. (Haddad, 2024; Birzeit University, n.d.; Farraj, 2024; Masharqa, 2025)

Ecocriticism, animal research and Gulf literature are on the rise. The Qatar University study established the Qatari case of “environmental novels” in the Dal Khalifa novel; the AUC's Alif 45 (2025) links literature, art, science, censorship and political prowess, with the theme “animal cultural expression”. (Belabed, 2023; Alif: Journal of Commercial Poetics, 2025)

Arabic AI studies began to shift from semantic accuracy to linguistic, dialectic, speech behaviour and teaching. AUC 2026 Master's thesis compares Gemini 2.5 Pro, GPT-4o and DeepThink-R1 with the interpretation of Egyptian television language, suggesting that “models translate” does not mean understanding irony, courtesy and media. (Ahmed, 2026) This is still a degree thesis, suitable for marking methodological trends and not for ranking as a model.

Sound/media archaeology and archival activation are not isolated themes. NYUAD Sawt al-Arshbef uses archival documents as a starting point, combining research articles, videos and family/custodian narratives; WMF ' s radio pioneer oral history and Gaza video research also incorporates voice lines, tone and sound design into narrative evidence. (Wømen and Memoory Forum, n.d.; Masharqa, 2025; and al Mawrid Arab Center for the Studi of Art, 2025)
21.2 Representing scholars-institutions-results

rows: Sarah Bowen Savant, Masoumeh Seydi, Maxim Romanov, etc.
Aga Khan University ISMC / KITAB / OpenITI

Converts the reference/copy/rewrite from individual discovery to a billion word-level relationship data while retaining text versions and alignment to trackability. (KITAB Project, n.d.; Smith et al., 2024; Nigst et al., 2025)
Authoritative Arabic, modern English translation, parallel text and free PDF; World Literature Dissemination and Versions. (Kennedy et al., n.d.)
General, National, and the Arabian Novel (2012); oral history/female archives
The rewrite of Arab novels by gender and the country's imagination are not limited to “women writers' studies”. (Elsadda, 2012; Elsadda, n.d.)
Hoda El-Saadi and others WMF Team
Oral history of women pioneers and actors
Production of counter-files with gender-sensitive interviews, biographical, audio material and public websites. (Wømen and Memoory Forum, n.d.)
Combining the sequences of 336 videos, character performances, humor, sound design, platform governance and emotional public. (Masharqa, 2025)
Kanafani, Trauma Memory and Palestinian Literature Research
Using magic reality, dispersion and trauma studies in Palestinian literature, the institutional pages can verify results and DOI. (Mohammad, n.d.)
The study of colonial trauma, family/political, individual/collective entanglements was conducted through interviews with writers and more detailed readings. (Haddad, 2024)

The hunger strike was placed in the system of practice of continued resistance by Palestinian prisoners, analysing rejection failures, political subjects generation and the liberation imagination. (Farraj, 2024)
Environmental novels study in Qatar (2023, DOI)
The introduction of ecological criticism into the Gulf Arab novels was also acknowledged as an area still in the early stages. (Belabed, 2023)
Comparative understanding of the Arabic LLM (2026)
Test speech behaviour with audio-visual television, and suggest “language benchmarking” for AI literary translation/dialogue studies. (Ahmed, 2026)
Regional art/media memory activated with archival articles, videos and custodians, emphasizing digital asset ethics. (al Mawrid Arab Center for the Study of Art, 2025)

blocks.text: 21.3 New theory/method

Text reuse network: Instead of automatically "copying" similar paragraphs, it is a combination of time, discretion, author community and book form that explains how memories flow. (Smith et al., 2024)
The relationship of Arab books: KITAB's “book” is a constant reorganization of existing texts, challenging modern single author-finalism. (KITAB Project, n.d.)

The gender-based paradigm is based on the analysis of male and female personalities in Elsadda, and the tracking of the country ' s projects to determine who enters the modern Arab novel history. (Elsadda, 2012)

Gender sensitive oral history: Treat interview relationships, silence, family/institutional power, anonymity and re-use permits as part of methodology, not as transparent facts. (Elsadda, n.d. ; Women and Memory Forum, n.d.)

Digital microstory/emotional public: research on the re-linking of short videos, performing personality, voice, humor, commentary/sharing and algorithmic visibility, linking narratives, media research and trauma studies. (Masharqa, 2025)

Emancipated Knowledge/Research: Birzeit focuses on local problems, participatory archives and anti-colonial theory production; the institutional situation of researchers must be taken into account in anti-reform. (Haddad, 2024; Birzeit University, n.d.)
21.4 Case study
Case ME-A: KITAB - “Books” as a network of memory that has been repeatedly reorganized

KITAB standardizes OpenITI text and cuts to about 300 word paragraphs, re-uses it with passim and publishes the alignment and statistics for each book; 2023.1.8 version of the data is about 10.2GB, DOI can track. The theoretical gain is that it can observe cross-century, cross-geographical quotations/rewriting; the risk is OCR/electronic text quality, standardized deletion of non-Arabic characters, and threshold selection that shapes “discovery”. Any macro-mapping should therefore go back to the matching and version sources for human verification. (KITAB Project, n.d.; Nigst et al., 2025)
Case ME-B: Women and Memoory Forum - from women to the rewriting of the archives

Elsadda's monographs do not add a female chapter to the existing classics, but rather demonstrate that the country-gender imagination is involved in the production of the classics themselves. (Elsadda, 2012) WMF further exposes oral history of female broadcasters, publishers, anti-Female Genital Mutilation activists, etc., to the research of voice, professional history and action networks. This approach turns “who is saved, who can describe, who has the right to re-use” into a research issue that can be read in conjunction with DREAMSEA, ALMEDA.
Case ME-C: Gaza Digital Teller - The Movement of Literature, Media and Witness Borders

Masharqa selects three non-professional youth lecturers to analyse 175, 103, 58 videos, defined as 2023-10-08 to 2025-01-19; focus on character creation, humor, hysteria, sound/clip and interaction. (Masharqa, 2025) This is not a quick reference to short video as “new literature”, but rather a representation that the subject of narrative research has crossed the print media. The study also exposed the meta deletions, algorithmic visibility and the loss of information; the replicatation study required the retention of time stamp, link status, transcribation/relay and ethical descriptions.
21.5 Arguments and asymmetries

Is ACO a “digital return” or a new centralization? It allows the Arab world to visit collections such as New York, but the selection remains influenced by copyrights in the public domain, cooperative collections and United States infrastructure. New York University Abu Dhabi Library, n.d.

Gulf funds promote high-quality projects such as ACO, LAL, al Mawrid, which will also create infrastructure advantages in conflict areas such as Abu Dhabi/Doha relative to Levant, Yemen and Sudan. (New York University Abu Dhabi Library, n.d.; Kennedy et al., n.d.; al Mawrid Arab Center for the Studi of Art, 2025)

Arabic is not a single low-resource language: the OCR/NLP gap between classical Arabic, modern standard language and dialects directly influences literary history conclusions. (Nigst et al., 2025; Ahmed, 2026)

The terms “genocide/settler colonialism/resistance” in the Palestinian study are legally, politically and academicly controversial; the archives should report accurately on the source terms, while distinguishing between the source claims and the analysis of the archives. (Birzeit University, n.d.; Masharqa, 2025)

Digital witness material involves trauma, minors, positioning and platform de-alignment; openness does not automatically amount to ethics, and secondary references should minimize harm and identifiable information. (Masharqa, 2025)
Chapter 22 Russia, Ukraine and Central and Eastern Europe: Decentralized shocks
22.1 Why can't the chapter on Russian/Eastern European literature continue?

The strongest academic change in the region after the full invasion in 2022 was the dismantling of the old structure of Russian literature as a default centre, and other cultures as an appendix to borders. The annual ASEEES 2023 conference, which was devoted to the theme “Decolonization”, openly called for reflection on the centrality of Russian curricula, institutions and research. The introduction of the socialist realism cluster of Slavic Review calls more specifically for a shift from self-contained “Russian culture/literacy” to imperial practice, former imperial borders, national identity and multiple local socialist realism; it also notes that the shift in space/anthropology has led to a shift away from a single normative model. (Dobrenko et al., 2023)

But “decolonization” is not an undisputed consensus. Kevin Platt reminds us that the colonial discourse must listen to the voices of the places/objects that are represented by others and understand the experience caught in multiple imperial relations with the use of “inter-imperiality”. (Platt, 2025) The 2026 paper by Ulrich Hofmeister, by comparing Ukraine with Russian Turkistan, advocates a careful distinction between colonialism and Russian, and opposes the obfuscation of different structures of domination as a single colonial relationship. The debate itself is at the forefront: the question is no longer “the ability to use post-colonial theories”, but rather how to build a concept that is historically distinguishable, politically inescapable and that allows the silence to speak out.

The second change is that literary research is directly linked to cultural survival. SUCHO launched distributed web-page preservation in March 2022; project files record the results of the thousands of websites and dozens of TB data, project papers/topic shorts to analyse volunteers, technology and ethics organizations. The third change is the dual track of computing literature: Polish DARIAH-PL, Czech poetry and DraCor continue to advance style measurement, literary geography, poetry and programmable theatres; the Russian classic Tolstoy Digital also shows the potential of words, but institutional environment, access, language centre and war ethics must be included in evaluation. (DARIAH-PL Consortium, n.d.; National Research University High School of Economics, n.d.; DraCoor Consortium, n.d.; Institute of Czech Literature, Czech Academy of Science, 2017)

rows: To centralize Russia, re-formulate the empire/colonial and discipline.

The Institute ' s theme, journal cluster, critical forum, 2026 conceptual and academic thesis collections together constitute ongoing debate (Association for Slavic, East European, and European Studies, 2023; Dobrenko et al., 2023; Hofmeister, 2026; Platt, 2025; Kravchenko et al., 2024)
War literature, Ukrainian literature and translation front
The literature guide for 2025 rewriting the history of literature from the current war in reverse and combining Ukrainian with Polish, Russian, Jewish, Crimean Tatar culture (Pavlyshyn, 2025)
Memory, trauma and national narrative production
Multiscale study of post-Chernobyl literature, urban ghost, Putin-era memory manufacturing and current war-forming (Hundorova, 2019; Blake, 2019; McGlynn, 2023)
Emergency digital humanities and cultural heritage preservation

SUCHO links preservation, forensics, collaboration and future research conditions; 2026 meetings continue to extend to data, poetry, plants, periodicals (Sucho), n.d.; Jolicoeur et al., 2022; Association for Computers and the Humanities, 2026)
Multilingual material, style measurement and open theatre/poemic infrastructure

DARIAH-PL, DraCore, R styrometry, Czech poetry and author attribution studies show methodological maturity (DARIAH-PL Consortium, n.d.; DraCoor Consortium, n.d.; Eder et al., 2016; Petr Plechá, 2021; Institute of Czech Literature, Czech Acadery of Science, 2017)

Annus, Tlostanova laid the comparative framework and the current controversy calls for modelling of the Baltic Sea, Ukraine, Central Asia and Russian colonies, respectively (Hofmeister, 2026; Annus, 2018; Tlostanova, 2017)
Environment, nuclear disasters, energy and war ecology
Chernobyl is a literary form/remember, with oil-modernity-war and ecological damage becoming a new chain (Hundorova, 2019; Etkind, 2023)
Central and Eastern European cities researched to deal with material traces and competitive memories of lost/displaced communities such as Jews, Poland, Ukraine and others (Blacker, 2019)
Important studies exist in the region, but the core evidence pool is dominated by the issues of war, decolonization and conservation; it cannot be inferred that these directions are inactive.

blocks.text: 22.3 Approach shift
22.3.1 History of the “Russia and its surroundings” to multicentrism, relationship-type literature

The old curriculum is often dominated by Russian literature, with the addition of “minority/Soviet national literature”. Dobrenko and Smola advocate studying the “near-speech” and non-synchronous life histories of socialist realism in different republics, regions and periods, which means that the comparator should change from a central prototype/local deviation to a multiple local occupation, consultation and deviation of the system. (Dobrenko et al., 2023) Pavlyshyn's guide, which is retrospected from the present reaction of Ukrainian literature, incorporates Polish, Russian, Jewish and Crimean Tatar culture, with the model “Ukrainian-centred but without a new monolingual history of closure”. (Pavlyshyn, 2025)

Methodological requirements: the language of the work, the self-identification of the author, the administrative boundaries, the place of publication and the acceptance of the Community code; the same author may belong to a number of literary networks; “Russian writing” cannot automatically be equated with “Russian literature”; the translation of names, place names and rules on transliteration shall indicate political/linguistic choices.
22.3.2 Rethinking from the application of post-colonial terminology to historical typologies and locations

Annus and Tlostanova have demonstrated that post-colonial/decolonization reveals modern hierarchies, frontier subjects and cultural resistance of the Soviet Union and post-socialism. The latest forum called for a second round of reflection: Hofmeister, which compares land, immigration, legal isolation and domination with the idea that “colonialism” cannot be used without distinction simply because of conquest; Platt, who demands to hear local subjects who are both affected by the Soviet Empire and in German/Po/Othman/Western imperial relations. (Hofmeister, 2026; Platt, 2025)

The study should establish a conceptual checklist: territorial conquest, settlement colonization, ethnic/legal differences, resource transfer, language repression, intellectual hierarchies, sovereign denial of separate evidence; and allowing different mechanisms to coexist but not to seek the same name. Authors are also required to report on their language skills, institutional location, war relations and archival competence.
22.3.3 From conventional digitization to emergency digital DH

The goal of SUCHO is not to complete the perfect collection, but to preserve evidence when the site is destroyed or altered within hours. (Sucho, n.d.) The project paper shows that volunteers need to discover web sites, prioritize, run grabs, examine documents, manage duplication, and coordinate language and technical skills. This makes “velocity-integrity-consent-security” a trade-off of methods. Web-page preservation may contain personal data, military sensitive information or content that communities are unwilling to disclose, and therefore must be separated from open access.

The “Emergence and Equity in Ukrainian DH” of ACH 2026 combines data, poetry, plants and periodicals, showing that the emergency approach is expanding from website rescue to war ecology, literary forms and research infrastructure. The agenda of the meeting is, however, a signal in progress and formal conclusions await the publication of papers/data.
22.3.4 From single trauma narrative to memory system, urban absence and environmental time

Hundorova explains how the nuclear catastrophe has changed the subject, ruin, body and post-modern form of Ukrainian literature by means of the “post-Chernobyl Library”; this is not a disaster to which every text of the 1990s was reduced, but rather an era of thought. (Hundorova, 2019) Blake analyses the “phantoms of the expelled/sacrifice communities” through architecture, memorials, literature and urban roaming; McGlynn examines how the country shapes its past through cultural production. (Blacker, 2019; McGlynn, 2023) The three combinations distinguish between traumatic experiences, physical traces, public memory and national mobilization, avoiding the “memory” being treated as one dimension.
22.3.5 From Black Box Style Attribution to Multi-Performance, Language Validation and Programming Texts

The stylo R package popularizes the most commonly used words, distances, clusters and classifications, but the results change with the subparagraphs, word numbers, standardization and composition of the language; the parameters scan and one validation cannot be omitted. (Eder et al., 2016) Plecháč for Henry VIII uses both high frequency and rhyme modes, and two types of relatively independent evidence are compatible sub-paragraphs, with the demonstration of multi-characterization cross-validation better than a single model assertion. (Peter Plecháč, 2021) The Tei/API of DraCor makes role networks and theatre structures available, while DARIAH-PL and Czech poetry language reminds that methods must be adapted to the language, poetry and local literature. (DARIAH-PL Consortium, n.d.; DraCoor Consortium, n.d.; Institute of Czech Literature, Czech Acadmy of Science, 2017)
22.4 Three deep case studies
Case A: Does decolonization apply to all Eastern Europe? - Designing the argument for test comparison

ASEEES and 2023 Slavic Review show that the full-scale war has made going to Russia central an unavoidable academic task. (Association for Slavic, East European, and European Studies, 2023; Dobrenko et al., 2023) However, Forum 2025-2026 has shown that political urgency cannot be a substitute for conceptual division. (Hofmeister, 2026; Platt, 2025) A reliable comparison should not begin with the classification of the minorities of Ukraine, the Baltic Sea, the Caucasus, Central Asia and Russia as the same colonies before looking for examples.

Operational options: two periods (late Russia, late Soviet Union) and four locations; indicators for land systems, population migration, administrative differences, language education, resource flows, elite absorption and sovereignty narratives; and mechanisms for interpretation of novels, newspapers, curricula and archives. Quantitative indicators are only responsible for showing differences, and literature is detailed to explain how domination is perceived, imitated, rejected or translated. The study could have concluded that “a number of colonial regimes coexist but of different types”, without the need to choose between “total colonization/total non-colonialization”.

The collection of papers by Kravchenko and Stech editors provides an interdisciplinary complement: Western research institutions have misjudged Ukraine for having a Russian central framework and are also the subject of research. (Kravchenko et al., 2024) The literature review should therefore be supported by statistical courses, posts, book classification and translation, and not just text analysis.
Case B: SUCHO - When is preservation a literary research method?

SUcho has been active since 2022-03-01 and the official page records the results of the stages of some 50 TB, 5,000 websites that were kept in mid-2022; the numbers will continue to change and the references must be timed. The significance of this is to view the web page, the museum catalogue, the local history, the digital exhibition and the website of the literary institutions as a source of evidence for the future cultural history. Project papers/topic shorts allow collaborative processes to examine: how global volunteers judge website values across languages, operating tools, referencing failures and avoid duplication; and how their columns do not serve as a guarantee at the level of rigorous peer review. (Jolikoeur et al. 2022)

The subsequent design of literary research can be “archival biography” of a destroyed local literary museum: comparison of pre-war pages, SUcho snapshots, social media, writer memories and post-war reconstruction; recording of which links/audio/interactive loss; and allowing local librarians to determine the level of openness of sensitive materials. A distinction must be made between the preservation of copies, legal ownership, public access and the academic invocation of four rights. A state of emergency does not provide a permanent exemption from consent and privacy.
Case C: DraCor-stylo - Czech Poetry - Central and Eastern Europe is not a passive inputer of the method of calculation

The Central and Eastern European team has been involved in the development of style measurement and language tools for a long time. Sylo allows researchers to systematically change the range, distance and sampling of the most commonly used words and observe cluster stability; Plecháč handles co-authors with vocabulary and rhythm; Czech poetry linguistics push calculations towards the language and rhythm history; and DraCor provides a cross-linguistic, dynamic version of TEI drama and API. (DraCoor Consortium, n.d.; Eder et al., 2016; Petr Plecháč, 2021; Institute of Czech Literature, Czech Acadmy of Science, 2017)

High-quality reproduction is available for 50 Czech, Polish, Ukrainian and Russian-language plays: the creation of role networks and a structured baseline in the language, then a comparative structure; the segregation of translations; reporting of the age, gender, publication/drama bias of each language. Tolstoy Digital can serve as a high-density syntax model, but treating a Russian classic project as a regional digital literature representative would re-establish the central structure that is being criticized. National Research University High School of Economics, n.d.
22.5 Themes map

rows: Wartime preservation, DARIAH-PL, DraCoor, semantic, style measurement and poetry (Saviing Ukrainian Cultural Heritage Online (SUCHO), n.d.; Jolicoeur et al., 2022; DARIAH-PL Consortium, n.d.; National Research University High School of Economics, n.d.; DraCoor Consortium, n.d.; Eder et al., 2016; Petr Plecháč, 2021; Institute of Czech Litoral, Czech Academy of Science, 2017)

From centralization to colonial mechanisms, trans-imperialism and local subject sounds (Association for Slavic, East European, and European Studies, 2023; Dobrenko et al., 2023; Hofmeister, 2026; Platt, 2025; Annus, 2018; Tlostanova, 2017)
The evidence pool did not form a regional cluster; the Polish/Czech/Russian language and reading experiment was also examined and could not be judged absent.

Post-socialist feminism, war gender and child culpability are important gaps; current sources are not sufficiently sequenced to recommend that the general report be clearly marked as search blind Sector
Chornobyl nuclear time, oil/modernity, war ecological damage and plant/data emergency studies feature (Hundorova, 2019; Etkind, 2023; Association for Computers and the Humanities, 2026)
Ukrainian literature enters the world through an English translation, while maintaining the political character of multilingualism and the front line of translation (Pavlyshyn, 2025).

DraCor has the potential to generate/smart interface research, and evidence pools 2026 remain predominantly conservation and traditional calculations; the use of British-American Ai hotspots should be avoided in lieu of local agendas (DraCor Consortium, n.d.; Association for Computers and the Humanities, 2026)

Wartime oral/website, multimedia loss, urban ghost and digital snapshots can be strongly intersected, but the draft does not have enough independent sound research results (Blacker, 2019; Saving Ukrainian Cultural Heritage Online (SUcho), n.d.)
The nuclear disaster, the war trauma and the displaced health are highly correlated, but the literature is not well-established (Hundorova, 2019)
The independent approach can be further supplemented by the often-as part of the war, national memory and post-Chernobyl body (Hundorova, 2019; McGlynn, 2023)

Nuclear disaster, absence of the city of World War II, creation of national memory and the current war constitute a competitive time layer (Hundorova, 2019; Blake, 2019; McGlynn, 2023; Pavlyshyn, 2025)

blocks.text: 22.6 Arguments, blind spots and research opportunities

The concept of decolonization had expanded: if any inequality was called colonization, the concept would lose its comparative power; if the colonial perspective was rejected because history was not fully in line with the overseas colonial model, it would also obscure conquest, linguistic repression and sovereignty denial. Mechanisms are needed to break down rather than to label. (Hofmeister, 2026; Platt, 2025)

The need for a central Ukraine should not be eliminated from Jewish, Crimean Tatar, Polish, Russian-speaking Ukraine and mixed identities. Pavlyshyn's multilingual cultural framework provides a more balanced entry point. (Pavlyshyn, 2025)

War’s current nature and evaluation ethics: Researchers cannot demand that war writing be in peacetime aesthetic or excuse text/evidence from criticism for political sympathy. The functions of testimony, mobilization, journalism, poetry and late literature should be distinguished.

Russian culture is the wrongs of the State apparatus: the research of the creation of national memory must preserve dissent, exile, ethnic minorities and the oppressed authors; in turn, the emphasis on cultural autonomy cannot deny the imperial nature of the classical system. (Dobrenko et al., 2023; McGlynn, 2023)

Emergency open security risk: Publicly preserved address, person, collection or uncut metadata may cause harm. The SUCHO-type project should have delayed opening, sensitivity classification and local governance. (Saving Ukrainian Cultural Helitage Online (SUCHO), n.d.; Jolicoeur et al., 2022)

(b) Recalibration of the computational material: the classical resources of Russian are usually more complete than Ukrainian, Belarusian, Roma and small languages; “data are abundant” is not regional representation. Language-specific results and gaps must be reported together. (National Research High School of Economics, n.d.; Dracor Consortium, n.d.)

The present draft is not uniform within the region: the sources are stronger in Ukraine, Russia, Poland, the Czech Republic and the Baltic Sea, and literature is inadequate in the Balkans, Hungary, Romania, Bulgaria, the South Caucasus and Central Asia; if the general report addresses Central Asia in a separate chapter, the borders should be clarified.

Immediate implementation directions: building Ukrainian - Crimean Tatar - Idish - Polish - Multi-List in the Russian language; network of translations/delegations/communications of wartime poetry; links SUCHO snapshots to bibliographies, oral history and material sites; compares different imperial mechanisms without pre-set uniform labels; studies relationships between oil, nuclear power, coal mine, war and literary modernity in the energy humanities; and establishes data hosting, co-authorship and safe reference protocols for diaspora scholars.
Part IV. Comprehensive analysis
Chapter 23 Data perspective: periodicals, institutions and disciplines
23.1 Move from “who gets far” to “what gets visible”

The institutional map of global literary research contains at least five types of nodes: periodicals and publishers decide what results are available for reference; universities and research centres organize disciplinary boundaries; digital infrastructure determines what materials are available for retrieval and computation; funding institutions decide whether long-term collaboration and maintenance is possible; translation, archives and public humanist organizations bring knowledge into different languages and communities. Concentrating these nodes into national papers would lose both theoretical contributions, local linguistic ecology and infrastructure rights.

The report consolidates 258 source records, removing DOI or duplicated title, resulting in approximately 246 stand-alone outcomes/projects. The source was not a sample of national quotas, but rather the establishment of retrospective evidence nodes for the subject of nine districts 11; the figure below therefore only presents the evidentiary composition of the report and cannot be interpreted as the true production of the districts. North America and Europe have a larger number of entries, partly from English and DOI visibility, and partly from cross-regional theoretical/methodological sources, labelled as “global” or “North America”; and Africa, South/South-East Asia and the Middle East have a high proportion of institutional project pages, reflecting the priority given to local infrastructure during the current round and uneven data from peer reviews.
Figure 8 Macro-regional composition of sources included in the present report and percentage of DOI, peer review or A-level sources; does not represent real regional production
23.2 Issues being organized in periodicals

The front line of the journal is not simply a “new list of topics”, but rather how the editorial system requires researchers to rewrite evidence. For example, digital and computational humanistic journals increasingly require data, codes, narratives and error analyses; literary theoretical journals reconnect technology with power through AI, environment, translation or decolonization. Four institutional developments were observed in the special theme and meetings of 2024-2026:

AI is entering the literary approach. Journals and copyright agencies began to demand disclosure of AI uses, author qualifications and sources of content generation; policies are still changing, academic reports should set models and dates, and not use global assertions such as “AI is legal/illegal”. (U.S. Copyright Office, 2025; UNECO Expert Group on Artificial Intelligence and Culture, 2025)

The environment humanities move from thematic to horizontal frameworks. Climate, blue humanities, energy, toxicity and multi-species issues are integrated into world literature, memory, health, sound and media research, and are no longer covered exclusively by ecological criticism. (Chakrabarty, 2021; DeLoughey, 2019; Liboiron, 2021)

The move from colonization to institutional auditing. Courses, archives, metadata, translation, open licences, co-authorship and data sovereignty are part of the evaluation project. (D'Ignazio et al., 2020; Fiormonte et al., 2022; Smith, 2021)

The methodology is transparent and is spilled over from the field of calculations. Archiving research also describes gaps and survival biases; participatory research describes consent and governance; cognitive experiments describes efficacy, stimulation and extrapolation; and explanatory papers increasingly require cross-references and locationality. (Beelen et al., 2025; Jacobs, 2015; Aslı Zengin, 2024)

However, the subject numbers and the presentation only proved editorial attention and did not prove that results had stabilized. For meetings/plans not completed in 2026, the report uses the “agenda signal” label; results are only obtained from official online journal articles, books and data sets. This distinction avoids the development of future plans as a fait accompli and the misperception of the advocacy capacity of large institutions as a theoretical influence.
23.3 Four new forms of institutions
23.3.1 Literary laboratories

The Stanford Literary Lab, the European Cognitive/Medical Humanist Team, the Korean Human Informatics and Japanese-Ablog AI project show that “laboratories” are not just a natural science imitation. It can organize common language, labels, models, abnormal readings and short-run demonstrations, and can organize common interpretations by experienced, clinical and literary researchers. The evaluation should focus on the issue and the contributing role, not on the size of the equipment or data.
23.3.2 Inter-linguistic Infrastructure Coalition

ElTec, CLS INFRA, DraCor, OpenITI/KITAB, Bichitra, DREAMSEA, ALMEDA, etc., link the versions, metadata, tools and long-term maintenance to the ecology. Their theoretical contribution lies in the process of making “comparability, works, books, languages and classics” visible. The risk is that standardization, Northern trusteeship, project-based inequality and low-resource languages are once again marginalized. The multilingual alliance should disclose language errors, uncomparable logs, servers and governance structures. (Fiormonte et al. 2022; Beelen et al. 2025)
23.3.3 Public/community humanities networks

Women's oral history, Aboriginal voice files, environmental senses, digital literature preservation and wartime web-sustained links researchers to custodians, authors, activists and the public. The results are not just a paper, but include access rules, local copies, teaching materials, archive descriptions and maintenance capabilities. The “opening” of such projects should be determined by risk and relationships, not by downloading as the only indicator of success. (Liboisron, 2021; Gabriels, 2022; Smith, 2021; Robinson, 2020)
23.3.4 Platform and law as external bodies

Model providers, social platforms, copyright systems, publishing groups and referral systems increasingly directly determine the way in which research subjects exist. Digital original works may disappear with the platform, model updates may change the results of research, platform deletions may change memory files and copyrights limit the sharing of language material. As a result, literary research cannot be based solely on the platform; terms, interfaces, content clearance, versions and business models need to be entered into the material spectrum. (U.S. Copyright Office, 2025; Dame-Griff, 2023; Smith et al., 2024)
23.3.5 Literary system is not the external context of work: universities, creative writing and publishing groups

John Gillory's Collection of Criticism: Organizations for Literary Research (*Professing Criticism: Essays on the Organization of Literary Studies*) considers professionalization, faculties, curricula, labour and the public sphere as conditions for the form of literary knowledge. (Guillory, 2022) Mark James McGurl; by Mark McGurl, Project Age: The Rise of Postwar Fiction and the Risk of Creative Writing* further explains that creative writing projects and public higher education are not just author resumes, but shapes teaching, evaluating and replicable novels. (McGurl, 2009) Dan Sinykin's " Great Fiction: how grouping changes publishing and American literature " (*Big Fiction: How Community Changed the Publishing Industry and American Literature*) changes editorial work, location and authorship by grouping archives, industry information, interviews and detailed readings. (Sinykin, 2023)

These three form the institutional chain of “training-evaluation-publication-flow-forms”. It can correct two kinds of reductions: it cannot be attributed to the market alone, nor can it be assumed that literature existed in its entirety before entering universities and publishing groups. Stronger research designs should combine course/workshop archives, manuscripts with editorial records, ownership changes, marketing subtexts and text forms, and make it clear that the United States sample cannot be pushed globally without comparison.
23.4 Six sources of data inequality
